# Alauda Container Platform Documentation

> ACP (Alauda Container Platform) product documentation covering platform features and operations

Documentation for Alauda Container Platform, built with Node.js and MDX.
Includes CRD definitions and comprehensive platform guides.


## ai

- [docs/en/ai/about.mdx](docs/en/ai/about.mdx): Introduces Alauda AI as a platform for large-model practices and MLOps workflows, including model storage and version management, model inference service publishing and operations, agent orchestration, and AI application development and release.

## appservice

- [docs/en/appservice/installation.mdx](docs/en/appservice/installation.mdx): Explains how to install Alauda Container Platform Data Services Essentials, the Data Services RDS Framework, and Application Services business plugins through Marketplace and OperatorHub. It lists prerequisites, target clusters, install modes, channels, version selection, installation locations, and manual upgrade strategy guidance for MySQL, Redis, Kafka, RabbitMQ, PostgreSQL, and Keycloak operators.
- [docs/en/appservice/kafka/kafka.mdx](docs/en/appservice/kafka/kafka.mdx): Introduces Alauda Streaming Service for Kafka as a Kubernetes-based distributed stream processing and messaging platform for transmitting and storing high-volume data. It highlights use cases such as log collection, event-driven architectures, real-time analytics, and data integration.
- [docs/en/appservice/keycloak/keycloak.mdx](docs/en/appservice/keycloak/keycloak.mdx): Introduces Alauda Build of Keycloak as a Kubernetes-hosted identity and access management solution. It covers authentication and authorization capabilities including OpenID Connect, OAuth 2.0, SAML 2.0, single sign-on, user federation, identity brokering, and social login.
- [docs/en/appservice/mysql/mgr.mdx](docs/en/appservice/mysql/mgr.mdx): Introduces Alauda Database Service for MySQL-MGR as a high-availability MySQL solution based on MySQL Group Replication. It focuses on Kubernetes-based automated deployment and management of MySQL Group Replication clusters.
- [docs/en/appservice/mysql/pxc.mdx](docs/en/appservice/mysql/pxc.mdx): Introduces Alauda Database Service for MySQL-PXC as a Kubernetes-based high-availability MySQL service using Percona XtraDB Cluster synchronous replication. It covers automated lifecycle management, creating new PXC instances, and scaling existing clusters.
- [docs/en/appservice/overview/architecture.mdx](docs/en/appservice/overview/architecture.mdx): Explains the Alauda Application Services architecture on Kubernetes, covering service components such as MySQL, Redis, Kafka, RabbitMQ, and PostgreSQL, common services such as parameter templates, inspection, alerts, and backups, operator controllers, CRDs, persistence, and external access. It also provides supported service versions, compatible storage classes, backup storage options, and default scheduling rules.
- [docs/en/appservice/overview/intro.mdx](docs/en/appservice/overview/intro.mdx): Introduces Alauda Application Services as a Kubernetes-based service management platform for developers and operations teams. It positions the product around rapid deployment, visual operations, and centralized management of enterprise application services.
- [docs/en/appservice/postgresql/pg.mdx](docs/en/appservice/postgresql/pg.mdx): Introduces Alauda support for PostgreSQL as a cloud-native PostgreSQL management solution built on the Zalando Postgres Operator. It covers CRD-driven lifecycle management, automatic failover, rolling upgrades, and backup restoration for production PostgreSQL on Kubernetes.
- [docs/en/appservice/rabbitmq/rabbitmq.mdx](docs/en/appservice/rabbitmq/rabbitmq.mdx): Introduces Alauda Streaming Service for RabbitMQ as a Kubernetes-hosted AMQP message broker. It emphasizes asynchronous communication between applications and support for decoupled, scalable, resilient distributed systems.
- [docs/en/appservice/redis/redis.mdx](docs/en/appservice/redis/redis.mdx): Introduces Alauda Cache Service for Redis OSS as a Kubernetes-based in-memory data structure service. It highlights strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, persistence, high availability, clustering, caching, message queue, and real-time analysis scenarios.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/alert-policy-mgt/functions/link_to_alert.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/alert-policy-mgt/functions/link_to_alert.mdx): Clarifies how Application Service alert management relates to the platform-wide alert center. It notes that Data Services and Platform Management alert policies are isolated by permissions and must be viewed, updated, or deleted from the same view where they were created.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/alert-policy-mgt/intro.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/alert-policy-mgt/intro.mdx): Introduces alert policy management for Application Service instances, including metric alerts, custom alerts, alert templates, real-time alerts, and alert history. It explains alert, pending, normal, silent pending, and silent states, and connects alert notifications to monitoring indicators and platform notification delivery.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/backup-mgt/functions/backup_list.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/backup-mgt/functions/backup_list.mdx): Covers the backup management page for Application Services, including viewing instance backup status by project and namespace, checking automatic backup state, success and failure counts, latest backup results, and storage locations. It also explains backup history, restoring backups to new or existing instances, and deleting backup records.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/backup-mgt/functions/register_s3.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/backup-mgt/functions/register_s3.mdx): Explains how to register external S3 storage for project backups, including required endpoint, access key, secret access key, and bucket name details. It covers storage availability checks, manual connectivity checks, storage list status, and selecting registered S3 storage for automatic or manual backups.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/backup-mgt/intro.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/backup-mgt/intro.mdx): Introduces the Backup Center in Application Services as a centralized place for cross-instance backup and recovery. It covers filtering backup records by component, namespace, and keyword, running backup and restore operations, viewing backup history, managing backup files, and registering, updating, or deleting external object storage credentials.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/functions/create_inspection_task.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/functions/create_inspection_task.mdx): Explains how to create inspection tasks for Application Service component instances through kubectl or the web console. It defines the Inspection CR fields for component type, target instances, history retention, manual or scheduled triggers, and cron-style schedules.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/functions/run_inspection_task.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/functions/run_inspection_task.mdx): Covers manual and scheduled execution of inspection tasks and where results appear in the instance details page, inspection reports, or task records. It includes an InspectionJob YAML example with labels, owner references, target instances, health status, start and stop times, and execution status fields.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/functions/update_inspection_task.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/functions/update_inspection_task.mdx): Explains how to update or delete inspection tasks using Kubernetes operations. It notes that only Inspection spec fields are editable, metadata and status are read-only, and deleting an Inspection automatically removes the one-to-one InspectionJob.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/Kafka/cpu.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/Kafka/cpu.mdx): Provides Kafka CPU utilization optimization guidance covering network and I/O thread counts, producer batching, consumer fetch behavior, compression tradeoffs, JVM heap settings, garbage collection pressure, and broker scaling to distribute topic load.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/Kafka/memory.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/Kafka/memory.mdx): Provides Kafka memory optimization guidance for broker message size limits, JVM heap configuration, consumer group sizing, topic and partition counts, and broker scaling. It focuses on reducing memory pressure from oversized messages, too many consumers, and excessive partitions or replicas on one broker.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/Kafka/rebalance.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/Kafka/rebalance.mdx): Explains Kafka rebalance causes and impacts, including duplicate consumption, consumer group instability, slow consumption, heartbeat timeouts, and max.poll.interval.ms behavior. It recommends controlling consumer startup and shutdown, tuning rebalance timeouts, avoiding long partition processing, using static membership, and monitoring rebalance frequency and duration.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/Kafka/storage.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/Kafka/storage.mdx): Provides Kafka storage space optimization guidance for keeping cluster storage usage around 50-70 percent. It covers log cleanup policy, retention hours and bytes, segment sizing, message compression algorithm tradeoffs, compression.type, and metrics for monitoring offsets, segment size, retention size, and retention duration.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/MySQL/io.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/MySQL/io.mdx): Explains MySQL and InnoDB I/O behavior, including buffer pool misses, dirty page flushing, redo and undo logs, binlogs, temporary tables, and DDL rebuilds. It recommends high-IOPS storage, tuning innodb_log_file_size and dirty page parameters, optimizing slow SQL and temporary table usage, avoiding full table scans, scheduling heavy DDL off peak, and splitting large transactions.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/MySQL/memory.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/MySQL/memory.mdx): Provides MySQL memory optimization guidance for total memory checks, top memory events, thread memory usage, and current allocation queries. It discusses risks from multi-statement SQL, buffer pool misses and dirty pages, temporary tables, MGR XCom cache, table cache, adaptive hash index, BLOB fields, and choosing parameter or specification changes.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/MySQL/rowlock.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/MySQL/rowlock.mdx): Explains InnoDB row lock concepts and how to diagnose row lock waits with Innodb_row_lock status metrics. It recommends reducing hot updates, using pagination or segmented processing, tuning lock wait parameters, printing deadlock details when needed, adding precise indexes, limiting transaction scope, and avoiding overly broad range locks.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/MySQL/storage.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/MySQL/storage.mdx): Provides MySQL storage utilization guidance for preventing write failures, backup failures, and slow expansion when usage approaches 80-85 percent. It covers binlog retention, index bloat, BLOB/TEXT and long VARCHAR fields, InnoDB table fragmentation, optimize table, temporary table space, and planning capacity before large DDL operations.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/MySQL/thread.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/MySQL/thread.mdx): Explains how active thread and connection counts reflect MySQL load and how excessive active sessions can cause SQL queues and crashes. It covers show status Threads metrics, slow SQL diagnosis, table_open_cache tuning, metadata lock blocking during DDL, row lock conflict indicators, and terminating or optimizing blocking sessions.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/RabbitMQ/mnesia.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/RabbitMQ/mnesia.mdx): Explains common RabbitMQ Mnesia database failures, including MNESIA_BASE permission problems, hostname or rabbit@hostname directory mismatches, replica communication failures, and cluster restart deadlocks. It recommends fixing write permissions, checking hostname changes, investigating replica logs, restoring cluster configuration, and using rabbitmqctl force_boot when needed.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/Redis/bigkey.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/Redis/bigkey.mdx): Explains Redis BigKey thresholds for strings, lists, sets, sorted sets, and hashes, and how BigKeys affect memory, performance, and availability. It recommends using redis-cli --bigkeys for detection and splitting large strings, hashes, lists, sets, or sorted sets with commands such as GETRANGE, SETRANGE, LRANGE, RPUSH, SMEMBERS, SADD, ZRANGE, and ZADD.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/Redis/cpu.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/Redis/cpu.mdx): Provides Redis high CPU troubleshooting guidance, including checking Redis info CPU counters, Pod CPU metrics, logs, process load, maxmemory, persistence settings, slowlog entries, request volume, hot keys, uneven cluster traffic, Lua scripts, and architectural options such as read replicas, sharding, or instance CPU scaling.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/Redis/memory.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_optimization/Redis/memory.mdx): Explains Redis memory usage monitoring and optimization with maxmemory, memory stats, Pod memory metrics, dataset and non-dataset memory overhead, replication backlog, key-value metadata, eviction policies, expire policies, key design, and avoiding BigKeys. It recommends keeping maxmemory within instance capacity and choosing policies such as LRU, LFU, random, TTL, or noeviction based on workload needs.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_policy.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/how_to/inspection_policy.mdx): Explains how scheduled inspection tasks use crontab expressions and why local times must be converted to UTC for Kubernetes. It covers minute, hour, day, month, and week fields, special characters such as comma, hyphen, asterisk, and slash, and common scheduling examples.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/intro.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inspection-mgt/intro.mdx): Introduces the Inspection Center for Application Services middleware, covering manual and scheduled inspections, periodic health checks, inspection reports, recent status overviews, anomaly diagnosis, optimization suggestions, and report export for further analysis or sharing.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inst-upgrade-mgt/functions/batch_upgrade.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inst-upgrade-mgt/functions/batch_upgrade.mdx): Explains instance upgrade control for Application Services, including viewing deployed Operators, selecting clusters in a multi-tenant project model, inspecting Operator deployment mode, scope, status, and version, viewing managed instances, and triggering batch rolling upgrades that move instances through Progressing back to Running.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inst-upgrade-mgt/intro.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/inst-upgrade-mgt/intro.mdx): Introduces Instance Upgrade Management for Application Services instances grouped by Operator and component type. It explains instance status, version, namespace display, patch-version notifications after Operator upgrades, batch upgrades during chosen maintenance windows, and the distinction between patch upgrades and major or minor version changes.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/intro.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/intro.mdx): Introduces Unified Operations Management for Application Services as a combined module for backup management, inspection management, alert management, parameter template management, and upgrade management. It highlights preset and custom parameter templates, backup filtering and storage control, automated inspections, alert filtering and history, batch upgrades, and Operator status visibility.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/para-template-mgt/functions/param-templates.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/para-template-mgt/functions/param-templates.mdx): Covers parameter template management for MySQL PXC, MySQL MGR, Redis, and Kafka. It explains platform preset and project custom templates, viewing template lists and details, creating templates with default parameter values, batch importing parameters, importing from documentation, using templates during instance creation, and exporting templates as text.
- [docs/en/appservice/unified-om/para-template-mgt/intro.mdx](docs/en/appservice/unified-om/para-template-mgt/intro.mdx): Introduces the parameter template module as centralized management for component parameter settings. It supports predefined and custom templates to standardize component configuration across scenarios and make instance setup more efficient.
- [docs/en/appservice/upgrade.mdx](docs/en/appservice/upgrade.mdx): Explains how to upgrade Alauda Container Platform Data Services Essentials manually from Marketplace and Cluster Plugins, and how the Data Services RDS Framework follows automatic or manual Operator upgrade strategy. It warns that ACP v3 to v4 upgrades should keep global and workload clusters aligned to avoid Data Services view display issues.

## configure

- [docs/en/configure/backup/application/backup-app.mdx](docs/en/configure/backup/application/backup-app.mdx): Explains how to create application backup schedules for Kubernetes resources and PVCs using Backup and Recovery. It covers prerequisites, repository selection, namespace scope, included and excluded resource filters, label selectors, one-time or scheduled backups, Velero behavior, hooks, manual execution, log export, and cautions around hostPath PVCs, database data, and data drift during backup.
- [docs/en/configure/backup/application/hooks.mdx](docs/en/configure/backup/application/hooks.mdx): Explains backup and restore hooks for custom commands that run in cluster containers during Velero backup or restore tasks. It covers pre and post exec hooks for Pods, hook fields such as namespace filters, resource selectors, commands, containers, onError, and timeout, plus restore initContainer and exec hook examples.
- [docs/en/configure/backup/application/repository.mdx](docs/en/configure/backup/application/repository.mdx): Explains how to create and delete backup repositories for application and configuration backups. It covers object storage region, addressing style, endpoint, bucket, folder prefix, access keys, TLS skip verification, CA certificates, and the risk of deleting a repository still referenced by backup schedules.
- [docs/en/configure/backup/application/restore-app.mdx](docs/en/configure/backup/application/restore-app.mdx): Explains how to run application restore tasks from existing backup records for accidental deletion, namespace migration, cross-cluster migration, and disaster recovery. It covers repository and backup file selection, namespace and storage class mapping, Retain PV claimRef cleanup, YAML mode, hooks, existingResourcePolicy update behavior, retry, log export, namespace import, and fixed IP reconfiguration after migration.
- [docs/en/configure/backup/application/restore-imagesync.mdx](docs/en/configure/backup/application/restore-imagesync.mdx): Explains image registry replacement for cross-cluster or cross-platform application recovery. It shows how to create a labeled ConfigMap in cpaas-system that maps old image repositories to new ones so restored workloads pull from the correct repository after migration or image synchronization.
- [docs/en/configure/backup/etcd.mdx](docs/en/configure/backup/etcd.mdx): Explains etcd backup and restore for ACP clusters using the Cluster Enhancer EtcdBackupConfiguration resource. It covers local and S3-compatible backup storage, schedules, retention, pausing, viewing backup records, creating S3 secrets, triggering backups, and a high-risk three-control-plane etcd disaster recovery procedure with snapshot restore, data distribution, kubelet restart, and health verification.
- [docs/en/configure/backup/overview.mdx](docs/en/configure/backup/overview.mdx): Provides an overview of ACP backup and restore for cluster state and applications. It distinguishes etcd, registry, logging, and monitoring backup from Velero-based application backup and restore, explains required Data Backup plugins, backup repositories, immutable OS considerations, and scenarios such as in-cluster recovery and cross-cluster application migration.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/about-hcp.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/about-hcp.mdx): Introduces Hosted Control Plane as a multi-cluster model where each cluster's control plane is containerized and hosted in a management cluster. It highlights lower resource consumption, faster cluster creation and upgrades, and better scalability for multi-cluster operations.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/acp-node-planning.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/acp-node-planning.mdx): Explains Kubernetes node role planning in ACP, including control-plane and worker labels, infra nodes, and custom role nodes for logging, storage, monitoring, or other specialized workloads. It covers labeling, tainting, verifying nodes, scheduling Pods with nodeSelector and tolerations, and caveats for OLM Operators and Cluster Plugins.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/etcd-encryption.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/etcd-encryption.mdx): Explains the etcd Encryption Manager for ACP clusters, including installation scope, AES-GCM encryption for secrets and configmaps, seven-day key rotation, retaining eight historical keys, hot reloading kube-apiserver encryption configuration, resource migration for re-encryption, relevant configuration files, and checking EtcdEncryptionConfig status.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/how-to/access-cluster-with-kubeconfig.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/how-to/access-cluster-with-kubeconfig.mdx): Explains supported ways to access ACP clusters with KubeConfig from ac, kubectl, Helm, client-go, or automation. It compares platform-downloaded long-lived KubeConfigs with token-based KubeConfigs, covers API token creation, cluster server and CA reuse, RBAC-bound permissions, dedicated automation accounts, lifecycle and revocation behavior, and validation commands.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/how-to/add_external_addr_for_global_registry.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/how-to/add_external_addr_for_global_registry.mdx): Explains how to expose the global cluster's Platform Built-in registry through an external domain so workload clusters can pull images when they cannot directly reach the internal registry address. It covers certificate prerequisites, TLS secret creation, ingress rules for registry API paths and auth token access, optional registry Service creation, certificate expiry monitoring, and crictl or podman pull tests.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/how-to/config_resource_manager_policy.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/how-to/config_resource_manager_policy.mdx): Provides a practical guide for enabling kubelet CPU ManagerPolicy, Memory ManagerPolicy, and Topology ManagerPolicy to improve latency and NUMA locality for Guaranteed Pods. It covers reservedMemory calculation, node cordon and drain, kubelet state cleanup, restart and uncordon steps, verification of cpu_manager_state and memory_manager_state, and terminology for CPU pinning and topology alignment.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/how-to/update_public_repository_credentials.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/how-to/update_public_repository_credentials.mdx): Explains how administrators update the built-in public-registry-credential cloud credential so clusters can pull images from the platform Public Repository. It covers downloading the organization authentication file from the customer portal and uploading it through Clusters and Cloud Credential in the Administrator console.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/immutable-infra.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/immutable-infra.mdx): Introduces immutable infrastructure for ACP clusters, where node configuration is baked into immutable operating system images and changes are applied by replacing nodes. It highlights consistency, reliability, simplified operations, and image-based lifecycle management.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/network/aws-eks-lb.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/network/aws-eks-lb.mdx): Provides supplementary AWS EKS load balancer guidance, defining EKS Classic Load Balancer, Network Load Balancer, Application Load Balancer, AWS Load Balancer Controller, platform load balancer, and service annotations. It recommends explicit annotations to avoid eks-clb defaults and explains how AWS-assigned LoadBalancer addresses become platform load balancer access addresses.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/network/aws-eks.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/network/aws-eks.mdx): Explains AWS EKS network initialization for ACP-managed imported clusters. It covers subnet tags, kubectl, Helm, AWS CLI, and eksctl prerequisites, OIDC provider association, IAM policy and service account setup, AWS Load Balancer Controller Helm deployment, creating Ingress and LoadBalancer Services with annotations, kubeconfig updates, AWS account ID lookup, subnet tagging, and TLS certificate creation.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/network/azure-aks.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/network/azure-aks.mdx): Explains Azure AKS network initialization after importing an AKS cluster into ACP. It covers default LoadBalancer support, optional Ingress Controller deployment through Azure Kubernetes Resources, creating ACP Ingress resources with webapprouting.kubernetes.azure.com, creating LoadBalancer Services, service annotations, and TLS secret preparation for HTTPS.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/network/google-gke.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/network/google-gke.mdx): Explains Google GKE network initialization for imported clusters, noting default LoadBalancer and managed GKE Ingress support. It covers creating ACP Ingress resources with the GKE Ingress Class, HTTPS certificate prerequisites, expected public IP assignment timing, LoadBalancer Services, annotations, and viewing Ingress resources in Google Cloud.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/network/huawei-cce.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/network/huawei-cce.mdx): Explains Huawei Cloud CCE network initialization for imported clusters, including default LoadBalancer and Ingress support. It covers recommended manual CCE Ingress Class selection, ELB annotations for shared or dedicated load balancers, an alternative default IngressClass YAML method, LoadBalancer Service creation, and TLS secret preparation.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/storage/aws-eks.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/storage/aws-eks.mdx): Explains how to initialize storage for imported AWS EKS clusters in ACP by creating and assigning EFS and EBS storage classes. It covers EFS and EBS CSI driver prerequisites, default class names such as efs-sc and ebs-sc, EFS File System ID configuration, ARM instance limitations for A1 nodes, project assignment, and available StorageClass parameters for EFS access points and EBS volume types.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/storage/azure-aks.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/storage/azure-aks.mdx): Covers storage initialization for Azure AKS clusters managed by ACP, focusing on assigning the default block storage and azurefile file storage classes to projects. It lists AKS-generated storage classes such as default, azurefile, managed-csi, and premium variants, notes azurefile permission limitations, and links to Azure CSI disk and file storage parameter references for creating custom classes.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/storage/google-gke.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/storage/google-gke.mdx): Describes storage setup for Google GKE clusters after import, including required GKE features, Cloud Filestore API enablement, quota planning, and assigning standard-rwx or standard-rwo storage classes to projects. It compares default Filestore and Persistent Disk classes, documents storage class parameters by linking to Google Cloud references, and provides troubleshooting for Filestore API permission, quota exhaustion, and Persistent Disk CSI topology failures.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/storage/huawei-cce.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/storage/huawei-cce.mdx): Explains how to initialize storage for Huawei Cloud CCE clusters in ACP by assigning default csi-disk block storage and csi-nas file storage classes to projects. It summarizes supported CCE storage class types including csi-disk-topology, csi-local, csi-obs, and csi-sfsturbo, and calls out quota, PVC count, region support, and account suspension errors that can block PVC creation.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/storage/overview.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/cloud-init/storage/overview.mdx): Provides a public cloud storage overview for EKS, CCE, AKS, and GKE clusters managed by ACP. It compares each provider's default file and block storage classes, RWO and RWX PVC support, expansion support, and snapshot support so administrators can choose the right storage initialization guide.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/how-to/annotate-platform-url.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/how-to/annotate-platform-url.mdx): Shows how to configure bidirectional connectivity after importing a cluster by annotating the imported Cluster API resource with `cpaas.io/platform-url`. It explains the need for a reachable platform domain, IP, and valid certificate so alerting and log collection components in the imported cluster can access the global cluster.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/how-to/configure-audit-for-standard-kubernetes.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/how-to/configure-audit-for-standard-kubernetes.mdx): Guides administrators through enabling Kubernetes API server audit logging on imported standard Kubernetes clusters whose control plane nodes are user-managed. It provides a version-aware audit policy, required kube-apiserver flags and hostPath volume mounts under `/etc/kubernetes/audit`, multi-control-plane cautions, and verification commands for checking generated audit logs.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/how-to/fetch-kubeconfig.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/how-to/fetch-kubeconfig.mdx): Explains how to collect the cluster token, API server endpoint, and CA certificate required to import an existing cluster into ACP. It emphasizes safe kubeconfig handling, creates a cluster-admin service account token in `cpaas-system`, warns about token security and expiration, and includes commands for extracting the selected API server and decoded CA data from kubeconfig.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/how-to/image-repo-trust.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/how-to/image-repo-trust.mdx): Provides node-level Containerd configuration steps for trusting an image registry that lacks HTTPS or a publicly trusted TLS certificate. It covers backing up `/etc/containerd/config.toml`, detecting Containerd version, configuring insecure registries for v1.4/v1.5 or v1.6 hosts.toml layouts, and restarting Containerd on every current and future node that pulls platform images.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/how-to/netcard-chart.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/how-to/netcard-chart.mdx): Explains how to make ACP monitoring collect traffic metrics from custom-named network interfaces that do not match the default `eth.*|en.*|wl.*|ww.*` patterns. It instructs administrators to find the workload cluster's Prometheus or VictoriaMetrics moduleinfo resource and add `valuesOverride` settings for `networkDevice` with a custom regular expression.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/alibaba-ack.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/alibaba-ack.mdx): Walks through importing Alibaba Cloud ACK managed or dedicated Kubernetes clusters into ACP for unified management. It covers version prerequisites, image registry address discovery and trust checks, obtaining temporary KubeConfig from Alibaba Cloud, filling the import form, checking connectivity, post-import network annotation, and known limitations for node addition, audit data, control plane monitoring, and certificates.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/aws-eks.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/aws-eks.mdx): Describes importing an existing Amazon EKS cluster into ACP, with AWS CloudShell used to prepare kubeconfig and run cluster information commands. It explains HTTPS registry requirements, generating EKS kubeconfig with `aws eks update-kubeconfig`, obtaining a separate import token, completing the ACP import workflow, configuring post-import networking, and initializing EKS ingress and storage after import.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/azure-aks.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/azure-aks.mdx): Explains how to import Azure AKS clusters into ACP using Azure Cloud Shell and a cluster-admin token rather than using public cloud kubeconfig directly. It covers version and registry prerequisites, import form fields, connectivity checks, network configuration, post-import ingress and storage initialization, AKS node security group considerations, node access options with node-shell or `kubectl debug`, and Azure ALB internal or external LoadBalancer examples.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/gcp-gke.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/gcp-gke.mdx): Guides administrators through importing Google GKE standard clusters into ACP, explicitly excluding Autopilot clusters. It covers Cloud Shell preparation, retrieving the API server address and Base64-decoded CA certificate, obtaining an import token, completing the ACP import workflow, post-import network configuration, and follow-up initialization for GKE ingress controllers and storage.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/huawei-cce.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/huawei-cce.mdx): Explains how to import Huawei Cloud CCE public cloud clusters into ACP, including prerequisites for supported Kubernetes versions, non-Turbo CCE clusters, registry reachability, and outbound access from CCE to the platform. It covers downloading KubeConfig from the CCE console, obtaining an import token, parsing KubeConfig in the ACP import form, checking connectivity, adding post-import network annotations, and follow-up ingress and storage initialization.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/openshift.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/openshift.mdx): Guides users through importing an existing OpenShift cluster into ACP for centralized management. It covers registry address discovery, CRI-O insecure registry configuration, global CoreDNS forwarding for the OpenShift base domain, kubeconfig or token-based cluster information collection, ACP import steps, post-import addon deployment, OpenShift audit profile updates, and limitations around node addition, audit data, control plane metrics, and certificate visibility.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/overview.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/overview.mdx): Serves as the entry page for importing managed clusters into ACP, linking to provider-specific import procedures. It directs users to Standard Kubernetes, OpenShift, AWS EKS, Google GKE, Azure AKS, Alibaba Cloud ACK, and Tencent Cloud TKE import guides.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/standard-kubernetes.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/standard-kubernetes.mdx): Explains how to import kubeadm-based standard Kubernetes clusters into ACP, including the distinction between cloud-managed and user-managed Kubernetes clusters. It covers compatibility prerequisites, registry discovery and insecure registry handling, cluster information collection, ACP import workflow, post-import audit collection setup, custom NIC monitoring, and a Containerd LimitNOFILE fix for distributed storage deployment failures.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/tencent-tke.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/import/tencent-tke.mdx): Provides the import procedure for Tencent Cloud TKE Dedicated and Managed clusters in ACP. It covers version and registry requirements, registry address discovery and HTTPS trust validation, downloading KubeConfig from Tencent Cloud, completing the ACP import form, connectivity checking, required post-import network configuration, and TKE limitations for node addition, audit data, control plane monitoring, and certificate information.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/overview.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/overview.mdx): Introduces ACP management of existing Kubernetes, OpenShift, EKS, and CCE clusters under a central platform. It explains the concept of managed clusters and contrasts importing a cluster, where ACP actively connects with supplied cluster information, with registering a cluster, where a reverse proxy in the target cluster initiates a secure tunnel to the platform.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/register.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/managed/register.mdx): Explains how to register a managed cluster by deploying a reverse proxy in the target cluster so it initiates communication with ACP. It covers version and registry prerequisites, 24-hour registration commands, public/private/platform registry options, custom NIC monitoring considerations, and the Containerd LimitNOFILE workaround needed before or after deploying distributed storage.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/nodes/in-place-os-upgrade.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/nodes/in-place-os-upgrade.mdx): Provides a detailed checklist for planning and verifying in-place operating system upgrades on ACP self-built cluster nodes. It defines support boundaries, excludes immutable and provider-managed clusters, covers pre-upgrade OS/kernel compatibility, conflicting package checks, draining, etcd backup and quorum validation for control plane nodes, post-upgrade runtime, kubelet, DNS, time, SELinux/AppArmor, swap, firewall, and workload recovery checks, and lists high-risk failure scenarios.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/nodes/node-add.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/nodes/node-add.mdx): Explains how platform administrators add control plane or compute nodes to existing on-premises ACP workload clusters. It covers node preparation, architecture and OS consistency, SSH and SOCKS5 proxy requirements, control plane sizing rules, node availability checks, execution progress visibility, and retrying failed node additions.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/nodes/node-management.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/nodes/node-management.mdx): Documents node operations available in ACP, including updating labels, stopping and resuming scheduling, evicting Pods, setting taints, batch label and taint management, device label assignment, virtualization scheduling switches, and deleting on-premises cluster nodes. It includes Kubernetes scheduling concepts such as labels, taints, tolerations, DaemonSet eviction behavior, and cleanup script handling after Linux node deletion.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/nodes/node-monitoring.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/nodes/node-monitoring.mdx): Explains how to view node monitoring data from a cluster's node details page when monitoring components are installed. It describes navigation, node switching, exporting PromQL expressions, storage partition aggregation, and the meaning of CPU, memory, storage, inode, system load, disk throughput, disk IOPS, network traffic, and packet rate trend charts.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/nodes/overview.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/nodes/overview.mdx): Introduces ACP node concepts for physical servers and virtual machines, including core components such as Kubelet, Kube-proxy, and container runtime. It defines control plane and compute node roles, high-availability control plane sizing rules, restrictions on adding or deleting imported-cluster nodes, and points to node availability checks plus supported OS and CPU architecture requirements.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/on-premises.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/on-premises.mdx): Provides the full workflow for creating an on-premises ACP cluster on user-provisioned machines. It covers node requirements, load balancing with hardware VIP or self-built keepalived VIP, global-to-workload cluster connectivity, image registry options, Kube-OVN, Calico, Flannel, and custom networking choices, node and SSH/proxy parameters, monitoring deployment, extended Kubernetes component arguments, execution progress, and project association after creation.
- [docs/en/configure/clusters/overview.mdx](docs/en/configure/clusters/overview.mdx): Summarizes ACP cluster management models, including platform-provisioned immutable infrastructure, user-provisioned infrastructure, Hosted Control Plane, connected public cloud or CNCF-compliant clusters, and tunnel-based connectivity. It compares infrastructure, node OS, Kubernetes ownership, automation level, and version compatibility expectations for ACP 4.3 and later versus ACP 4.2 and earlier.
- [docs/en/configure/feature_toggles.mdx](docs/en/configure/feature_toggles.mdx): Explains how administrators use the ACP Web Console feature gate page to enable or disable platform capabilities. It covers accessing the `/console-platform/feature-gate` URL, disabling all Alpha or Beta features through stage settings, enabling features for a single cluster, and disabling specific features globally through system settings.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_alb.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_alb.mdx): Documents the deprecated ACP ALB custom resource stack and recommends ingress-nginx-operator or Envoy Gateway as replacements. It explains ALB2, Frontend, Rule, and Ingress sync resources, project and port-project assignment, host or container network modes, VIP and LoadBalancer service settings, listener protocols, DSLX matching, backend service groups, TLS edge and re-encrypt modes, rule actions, ingress-to-rule translation, default SSL strategy, and ALB logs and monitoring.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_certificate.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_certificate.mdx): Explains how platform administrators create TLS certificate resources in ACP and assign them to all projects, selected projects, or no project initially. It describes how developers use allocated certificates for ingress and load balancing, notes that public cloud clusters should use Kubernetes TLS Secrets instead, and specifies public key `tls.crt` and private key `tls.key` input requirements.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_coredns.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_coredns.mdx): Describes updating the Alauda Build of CoreDNS cluster plugin from the ACP Marketplace. It covers configuring host aliases for custom DNS records, node selectors for CoreDNS Pod placement, and tolerations so CoreDNS can run on tainted nodes.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_domain.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_domain.mdx): Explains how to create and allocate domain resources in ACP for cluster-wide or project-specific use by ingress rules, native applications, and load balancer listener ports. It covers full and wildcard domains, DNS resolution prerequisites, optional certificate binding, Domain custom resources, web console and CLI creation, and using the same `spec.name` domain across multiple clusters through distinct Domain resources and cluster labels.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_gatewayapi_gateway.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_gatewayapi_gateway.mdx): Provides a comprehensive guide to creating Gateway API `Gateway` resources backed by Envoy Gateway in ACP. It explains Gateway listeners, GatewayClass selection, companion `EnvoyProxy` deployment configuration, LoadBalancer, NodePort, and ClusterIP exposure, MetalLB VIP annotations, allowed route namespaces, TLS terminate and passthrough modes, web console fields, YAML examples with HTTP, HTTPS, TLS, TCP, and UDP listeners, hostname intersection behavior, and supported route kinds per listener protocol.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_gatewayapi_policy.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_gatewayapi_policy.mdx): Explains Envoy Gateway policy attachment in ACP using `.spec.targetRefs` for `SecurityPolicy`, `BackendTLSPolicy`, `ClientTrafficPolicy`, and `BackendTrafficPolicy`. It maps supported targets and web console support, covers API key authentication, CORS, backend TLS validation with CA certificates or well-known CAs, client-facing timeout behavior, backend connection and request timeouts, sectionName targeting for Gateway listeners or Service ports, and YAML examples for each policy type.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_gatewayapi_route.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_gatewayapi_route.mdx): Documents creation and management of Gateway API `Route` resources in ACP after a Gateway is available. It covers HTTPRoute, TCPRoute, UDPRoute, GRPCRoute, and TLSRoute web console fields and YAML examples, listener attachment rules, hostnames, weighted backend services, HTTP matches, filters, request redirects, URL rewrites, CORS, timeouts, retries, session persistence, gRPC matches and header filters, TLSRoute passthrough behavior, and the ACP topology view for HTTPRoute policy dependencies.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_ingress.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_ingress.mdx): Explains how Kubernetes Ingress exposes HTTP and HTTPS routes from outside the cluster to internal Services in ACP. It covers ingress controller dependency, duplicate domain/protocol/path restrictions, port 80 and 443 behavior, ingress-nginx and ALB controller options, a sample Ingress manifest, and Web Console fields for IngressClass, allocated domains, certificates, path match type, backend Service, and Service port.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_metallb.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_metallb.mdx): Guides administrators through configuring MetalLB external IP address pools and BGP peers in ACP. It covers L2 and BGP pool types, CIDR and range inputs, available node selection by name or label, BGP peer parameters such as local and peer AS numbers, local and peer IPs, ports, multihop, and router ID, CLI examples for IPAddressPool with L2Advertisement or BGPAdvertisement, and common troubleshooting for missing external IPs, RBAC issues, failed BGP sessions, and L2 reachability.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_node_local_dns.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_node_local_dns.mdx): Explains installation and operation of the Alauda Build of NodeLocal DNSCache plugin to reduce DNS latency and CoreDNS load by running a local DNS cache on each node. It covers Marketplace installation, recommended IPv4 and IPv6 cache IP ranges, Kube-OVN Underlay incompatibility, kubelet restarts, new-Pod-only effect, Kube-OVN controller parameter requirements, NetworkPolicy allowances, MicroOS upgrade persistence, 4.2.x upgrade handling, and example NetworkPolicy rules for DNS traffic.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_service.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_service.mdx): Documents Kubernetes Service creation and usage in ACP for stable access to Pods. It explains ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, ExternalName, and headless Services, Web Console fields for virtual IPs, target components, label selectors, service and container ports, protocols, and session affinity, CLI examples with `kubectl apply` and `kubectl expose`, internal and external nginx access demos, cloud-provider LoadBalancer annotations for EKS, CCE, AKS, and GKE, and a MetalLB BGP LoadBalancer example using `externalTrafficPolicy: Local`.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_subnet.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/functions/configure_subnet.mdx): Explains subnet creation and allocation rules for ACP networking with Calico and Kube-OVN. It covers project and namespace IP allocation behavior, Calico IPIP, VXLAN, and no-encapsulation modes, CIDR-to-blockSize mapping, Kube-OVN Overlay subnet settings such as reserved IPs, distributed or centralized gateways, NAT, and ECMP, plus Kube-OVN Underlay workflows for ProviderNetwork bridge networks, VLAN resources, physical gateway subnets, and project or namespace assignment.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/alb/tasks_for_alb.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/alb/tasks_for_alb.mdx): Provides ALB operational patches for scheduling ALB-related components on selected nodes. It shows how to set nodeSelector and tolerations for the ingress-nginx-operator subscription and how to patch an `ALB2` resource with `alb.cpaas.io/toleration` annotations and `spec.config.nodeSelector` settings.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/configure_endpoint_health_checker.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/configure_endpoint_health_checker.mdx): Explains the ACP Endpoint Health Checker plugin, which actively monitors service endpoints and removes unhealthy endpoints to improve failover during node outages. It covers Marketplace installation with node selectors and tolerations, readiness and liveness probe integration, TCP health checking, ALB plus MetalLB failover improvements from roughly 40 seconds to 10 seconds, enabling checks with pod annotations for ALB2, IngressNginx, Envoy Gateway, and custom Deployments, legacy readinessGates, and uninstallation.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/config_metallb_underlay.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/config_metallb_underlay.mdx): Provides a step-by-step solution for using MetalLB L2 mode with Kube-OVN Underlay networking so LoadBalancer VIPs come from the same Underlay subnet as backend Pods. It covers ACP version and IPv4 constraints, VLAN sub-interface ProviderNetwork configuration, Kube-OVN controller settings `Skip CT for Dst LPort IPs` and `Enable OVN LB Local`, enabling `enableExternalLBAddress` and reserving VIP ranges, creating MetalLB IPAddressPool and L2Advertisement resources, deploying a LoadBalancer Service with `externalTrafficPolicy: Local`, verification, and migration of existing Services to the Underlay pool.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/configure_bgp.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/configure_bgp.mdx): Explains how Kube-OVN advertises Pod and Subnet routes to external networks with BGP using `kube-ovn-speaker` and GoBGP. It covers labeling speaker nodes, matching the speaker manifest to the Kube-OVN version, configuring one or multiple upstream peers with neighbor addresses and AS numbers, enabling route advertisement through `ovn.kubernetes.io/bgp` annotations on Pods or Subnets, disabling NAT for exported Subnets, Cluster versus Local announcement policies, and advanced speaker flags such as authentication, hold time, graceful restart, passive mode, and EBGP multihop.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/configure_centralized_gateway.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/configure_centralized_gateway.mdx): Explains how to configure Kube-OVN centralized gateway mode so Pods in a subnet use fixed node IPs for outbound access, auditing, whitelisting, and firewall control. Covers Subnet fields such as `gatewayType`, `gatewayNode`, `gatewayNodeSelectors`, `natOutgoing`, and `enableEcmp`, plus ECMP and primary-backup failover behavior.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/configure_egress_gateway.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/configure_egress_gateway.mdx): Covers Kube-OVN VPC Egress Gateway for controlling Pod egress through static external addresses with SNAT, ECMP load sharing, optional BFD failover, selectors, and subnet or IP block policies. Includes creating a NetworkAttachmentDefinition and external subnet, defining `VpcEgressGateway`, checking routes, iptables, tcpdump, OVN logical router policies, scaling replicas, enabling VPC BFD ports, and tuning gateway fields such as internal/external IPs, tolerations, selectors, and traffic policy.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/configure_ippool.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/configure_ippool.mdx): Describes Kube-OVN `IPPool` as a finer-grained IPAM unit inside a Subnet for subdividing address ranges and binding them to namespaces. Shows IP range formats, namespace binding, Pod and workload annotations via `ovn.kubernetes.io/ip_pool`, and operational constraints such as non-overlapping pools, reserved IP inheritance, and exclusion of pool ranges from general subnet allocation.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/configure_mtu.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/configure_mtu.mdx): Explains Kube-OVN MTU behavior for overlay and underlay networks, including automatic physical NIC detection and encapsulation overhead calculations for Geneve and VXLAN over IPv4 or IPv6. Covers global MTU configuration on the `kube-ovn-cni` DaemonSet, per-Subnet `mtu` overrides, and the need to recreate Pods after increasing MTU because OVS internal ports adopt the smallest bridge interface MTU.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/configure_ovn_interconnection.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/configure_ovn_interconnection.mdx): Guides operators through Kube-OVN cluster interconnection for Pod-to-Pod access across clusters in the default VPC, including prerequisites for non-overlapping CIDRs and reachable controller/gateway nodes. Covers deploying the OVN interconnect database by Deployment, Podman, or containerd, configuring the global `ovn-ic` ConfigMap, joining and leaving clusters through the console, cleanup scripts for stale interconnect data, uninstall steps, and configuring high-availability gateway nodes.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/kubeovn_underlay_py.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/kubeovn_underlay_py.mdx): Prepares the physical network for Kube-OVN Underlay mode, where Pod traffic depends on external switching, VLANs, gateways, and a dedicated bridged NIC. Details dual-NIC requirements, VLAN terminology, switch and gateway configuration, ICMP gateway checks, hairpin and virtualization settings, unsupported public cloud environments, and commands for testing VLAN subinterface connectivity before platform configuration.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/multiple_networks.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/multiple_networks.mdx): Shows how to use Multus CNI with Kube-OVN so Pods and workload controllers can attach secondary network interfaces backed by Kube-OVN IPAM. Covers installing the Alauda Multus plugin, creating a NetworkAttachmentDefinition and matching Kube-OVN Subnet provider, annotating Pods or Deployment templates with `k8s.v1.cni.cncf.io/networks`, and verifying allocated IP, MAC, gateway, and network-status annotations or in-Pod interfaces.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/underlay_overlay_st.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/underlay_overlay_st.mdx): Explains automatic interconnection between Kube-OVN Underlay and Overlay subnets by enabling `u2oInterconnection` on an Underlay Subnet. Includes console steps, Pod recreation requirements, and isolation guidance using kube-ovn-controller `--ls-ct-skip-dst-lport-ips=false` plus Subnet ACL rules with `to-lport` direction, OVN match expressions, priorities, and conntrack considerations.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/understanding_kube_ovn.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/kube_ovn/understanding_kube_ovn.mdx): Introduces Kube-OVN architecture and how it maps Kubernetes networking concepts onto OVN/OVS SDN primitives such as logical switches, routers, VPCs, ACLs, gateways, QoS, and traffic mirroring. Describes `ovn-central`, `ovs-ovn`, `kube-ovn-controller`, `kube-ovn-cni`, Pod annotation-based IPAM flow, and operational components including `kube-ovn-speaker`, `kube-ovn-pinger`, `kube-ovn-monitor`, and `kubectl-ko`.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/soft_data_center_lb_solution.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/soft_data_center_lb_solution.mdx): Provides an alpha software data center load balancer design using external host nodes running Keepalived and IPVS to load-balance traffic for multiple ALBs with IPv4, IPv6, or dual-stack VIPs. Covers host prerequisites, kernel modules, sysctl settings, `alive.yaml` VIP and backend configuration, kubeconfig certificate handling, container runtime launch with `nerdctl`, and verification through `ipvsadm`, VIP failover, and `curl` tests.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/task_for_migrate_from_ocp_route_to_gatewayapi_route.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/task_for_migrate_from_ocp_route_to_gatewayapi_route.mdx): Maps OpenShift Route features to Kubernetes Gateway API and Envoy Gateway resources for migration planning. Covers equivalent HTTPRoute, Gateway, TLSRoute, BackendTLSPolicy, ClientTrafficPolicy, BackendTrafficPolicy, and SecurityPolicy configurations for host routing, timeouts, HSTS, session affinity, path matching, header modification, connection and rate limits, IP allow or deny lists, URL rewrites, cross-namespace routing, default certificates, re-encrypt, edge termination, passthrough, testing, DNS cutover, and rollback.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/tasks_for_envoy_gateway.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/tasks_for_envoy_gateway.mdx): Collects advanced Envoy Gateway tasks beyond the main Gateway API configuration path, including OpenTelemetry, cross-namespace route attachment, cross-namespace certificate references with ReferenceGrant, TLS passthrough, minimum TLS version, NodePort and MetalLB VIP control, Pod annotations, and nodeSelector or toleration settings. Also covers hostNetwork operation with port offset or privileged listener ports and how to handle in-cluster access to LoadBalancer VIPs when `externalTrafficPolicy` is Local.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/tasks_for_ingress_nginx.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/how_to/tasks_for_ingress_nginx.mdx): Summarizes operational tasks for the ingress-nginx operator, linking key NGINX settings for max connections, timeouts, sticky sessions, header modification, rewrite, HSTS, rate limiting, WAF, forwarded prefix headers, TLS re-encrypt, edge termination, passthrough, and default certificates. Provides concrete guidance for preserving client source IP through HAProxy PROXY protocol or MetalLB with `externalTrafficPolicy: Local`, including configuration snippets and placement requirements.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/trouble_shooting/arm_checksum.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/trouble_shooting/arm_checksum.mdx): Troubleshoots Kube-OVN Overlay inter-node communication failures on ARM environments caused by incorrect NIC checksum offload behavior with older kernels and some domestic network cards. Recommends upgrading to kernel 4.19.90-25.16.v2101 or later, or disabling transmit checksum offload with `ethtool -K eth0 tx off` as a mitigation.
- [docs/en/configure/networking/trouble_shooting/find_who_cause_the_error.mdx](docs/en/configure/networking/trouble_shooting/find_who_cause_the_error.mdx): Explains how to identify ALB error sources by inspecting the `X-ALB-ERR-REASON` response header. Lists meanings for `InvalidBalancer`, `BackendError`, and `InvalidUpstream`, distinguishing missing service endpoints, backend-originated responses, and unmatched routing rules that produce ALB 404 errors.
- [docs/en/configure/notification/cluster_notification.mdx](docs/en/configure/notification/cluster_notification.mdx): Explains how to install and configure the Alauda Container Platform Cluster Notification plugin for workload-cluster notification delivery. Covers required ACP and plugin versions, downloading from AlaudaCloud, pushing packages with `violet`, installing through the Marketplace or `ModuleInfo` YAML, and creating a base64-encoded `NotificationServer` Secret for email settings such as host, port, sender, SSL, username, password, and certificate verification.
- [docs/en/configure/scalability/disk_configuration.mdx](docs/en/configure/scalability/disk_configuration.mdx): Provides disk capacity and performance guidance for ACP clusters, especially etcd, containerd, `/cpaas`, root filesystem, and installer package storage. Recommends SSD or NVMe for etcd, dedicated disks or logical volumes, avoiding network-backed storage for etcd, and validating sequential write latency and IOPS with a `fio` benchmark that checks p99 fsync latency against a 10 ms threshold.
- [docs/en/configure/scalability/evaluating_global.mdx](docs/en/configure/scalability/evaluating_global.mdx): Gives resource-sizing guidance for ACP global clusters managing multiple workload clusters. Defines small through extra-large tiers by managed cluster count, nodes, CPU, and memory, then explains vertical and horizontal scaling triggers, monitoring targets for CPU, memory, API latency, and etcd commit latency, and Prometheus queries for validating capacity.
- [docs/en/configure/scalability/evaluating_workload.mdx](docs/en/configure/scalability/evaluating_workload.mdx): Provides control-plane sizing and tested scalability guidance for ACP workload clusters. Covers recommended CPU and memory by worker-node and namespace density, headroom during failures or upgrades, observed maximums for nodes, Pods, namespaces, Services, Secrets, ConfigMaps, deployments, and CRDs, plus scaling factors such as Pod churn, network policies, API request rate, and resource consumption.
- [docs/en/configure/scalability/large_clusters_stability.mdx](docs/en/configure/scalability/large_clusters_stability.mdx): Lists practical stability recommendations for large Kubernetes clusters focused on reducing control-plane pressure and blast radius. Covers etcd disk I/O and database size, hot key churn, per-resource data size, API server load balancer capacity, Service count limits, NodeLocal DNSCache, reducing Pod update storms, disabling unneeded service account token mounts, limiting object history, aligning requests and limits, and monitoring controller restarts.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/concepts/key_concepts.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/concepts/key_concepts.mdx): Introduces Kubernetes storage building blocks used by ACP, including PersistentVolumes, PersistentVolumeClaims, StorageClasses, CSI drivers, generic ephemeral volumes, `emptyDir`, `hostPath`, ConfigMaps, and Secrets. Explains how PVCs request storage, StorageClasses drive dynamic provisioning, CSI controller and node components create and mount volumes, and administrators expose storage tiers while developers consume them declaratively.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/concepts/pv.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/concepts/pv.mdx): Explains PersistentVolumes as cluster-level abstractions for backend storage with lifecycles independent of Pods. Compares dynamic PVs provisioned through StorageClasses with static PVs created by administrators, including HostPath for single-node testing and NFS shared storage, and outlines provisioning, binding, use, release, and reclaim stages.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/concepts/selection.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/concepts/selection.mdx): Explains Kubernetes PVC access modes and volume modes and how ACP storage classes support them. Compares RWO, ROX, and RWX support across CephFS, CephRBD, TopoLVM, and NFS, describes Filesystem versus Block volume modes, and identifies which storage classes support snapshots and expansion for workload planning.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/cosi/cosi-concepts.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/cosi/cosi-concepts.mdx): Introduces Container Object Storage Interface concepts for Kubernetes administrators by mapping BucketClass, Bucket, and BucketClaim to familiar StorageClass, PV, and PVC ideas. Describes dynamic bucket provisioning, driver and deletion policy selection, generated credential Secrets, Pod consumption, and the workflow from administrator-created BucketClass to namespace-scoped BucketClaim binding.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/cosi/functions/bucket_class_ceph.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/cosi/functions/bucket_class_ceph.mdx): Guides administrators through creating a COSI BucketClass for Ceph RGW-backed object storage. Covers preparing internal Rook-managed or external Ceph, installing ACP COSI and COSI for Ceph plugins, generating or manually creating RGW credential Secrets with required UI labels, creating BucketClass through the console or YAML with `ceph.objectstorage.k8s.io`, and verifying it before creating Bucket or BucketClaim resources.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/cosi/functions/bucket_class_minio.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/cosi/functions/bucket_class_minio.mdx): Shows how to create a COSI BucketClass for MinIO S3-compatible object storage. Covers prerequisites for a ready MinIO cluster and ACP COSI plugins, creating a case-sensitive credential Secret containing `Endpoint`, `AccessKey`, and `SecretKey` with `app=minio` for UI discovery, and defining a `minio.objectstorage.k8s.io` BucketClass through the console or YAML with deletion policy and project allocation.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/cosi/functions/bucket_request.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/cosi/functions/bucket_request.mdx): Explains how users create a Bucket Request, or BucketClaim, to dynamically provision and bind an object-storage bucket from an Object Storage Class. Covers required COSI and backend plugins, the console path under Storage > Bucket Claims, selecting the Object Storage Class, waiting for `Available`, and the caution that deleting the bucket policy clears all bucket data.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/cosi/how_to/access_control_ceph.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/cosi/how_to/access_control_ceph.mdx): Shows how to combine CephObjectStoreUser, BucketClass, BucketClaim, BucketAccessClass, and BucketAccess to enforce least-privileged access and quotas for Ceph RGW-backed COSI buckets. Covers COSU capabilities and RGW quotas, driver credentials, BucketClaim provisioning, read-only, write-only, read-write, and anonymous read access policies, per-workload credential Secrets, quota updates, troubleshooting, rotation, and cleanup.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/cosi/install.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/cosi/install.mdx): Describes installing and uninstalling ACP COSI cluster plugins. Explains package download and upload prerequisites, installing the core Alauda Container Platform COSI plugin before the Ceph or MinIO backend plugin through Marketplace > Cluster Plugins, cluster-scoped installation constraints, status checks, and the required uninstall order for backend plugins before removing the core COSI plugin.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/cosi/intro.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/cosi/intro.mdx): Introduces COSI as a Kubernetes-native, declarative framework for managing object storage such as AWS S3, MinIO, and Ceph RGW. Highlights standardized provisioning of buckets, dynamic lifecycle management, Kubernetes Secret distribution of credentials, portability across cloud and on-premises backends, and current alpha limitations including support only for Ceph RGW and MinIO drivers.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/functions/cephfs_storageclass.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/functions/cephfs_storageclass.mdx): Explains how administrators create a CephFS file storage StorageClass backed by ACP distributed Ceph storage and CSI. Covers deploying the Ceph volume plugin in internal or external mode, then configuring reclaim policy, RWO or RWX access modes, project allocation, storage cluster, and storage pool so PVCs can dynamically provision shared file PVs for application file sharing and backup scenarios.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/functions/cephrbd_storageclass.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/functions/cephrbd_storageclass.mdx): Guides creation of a CephRBD block storage StorageClass for high-IOPS, low-latency workloads such as databases and virtualization. Covers deploying the Ceph volume plugin, selecting CephRBD Block Storage, configuring filesystem type, reclaim policy, ReadWriteOnce access mode, project assignment, storage cluster, and storage pool so PVCs can dynamically provision block PVs.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/functions/create_pv.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/functions/create_pv.mdx): Describes manual creation of static PersistentVolumes using HostPath or NFS Shared Storage. Includes prerequisites for backend capacity and access details, a sample PV manifest, web console and CLI creation flows, HostPath and NFS parameters, access modes, reclaim policies, and related operations for deleting unbound or retained PVs without losing required data.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/functions/create_pvc.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/functions/create_pvc.mdx): Explains how developers create PersistentVolumeClaims through the console or YAML to request storage from dynamic StorageClasses or statically bind to existing PVs. Covers namespace storage quota, PVC fields such as name, storage class, access mode, capacity, volume mode, labels, annotations, selectors, Bound status, workload binding, snapshot-based PVC creation, deletion precautions, and expanding PVC capacity with the console or `kubectl patch`.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/functions/nfs_storageclass.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/functions/nfs_storageclass.mdx): Shows how to deploy the ACP NFS CSI plugin and create an NFS Shared StorageClass for dynamically provisioned PVCs. Covers NFS server prerequisites, supported protocol versions, plugin installation through the console or YAML, StorageClass fields such as service address, path, protocol, reclaim policy, access modes, allocated projects, and `subDir` naming rules with uniqueness and cleanup caveats.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/functions/snapshot_con.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/functions/snapshot_con.mdx): Explains deployment of the ACP Snapshot Management plugin to enable CSI volume snapshots for dynamically provisioned PVCs. Covers snapshot concepts, support limitations, creating PVCs from snapshots, supported access modes for TopoLVM, CephRBD, and CephFS snapshot restores, and installation through Marketplace or a non-config `snapshot` cluster plugin YAML.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/functions/topolvm_storageclass.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/functions/topolvm_storageclass.mdx): Guides administrators through creating a TopoLVM local block StorageClass for high-performance, node-local storage. Describes TopoLVM advantages over remote storage and hostPath, suitable use cases and data-loss limitations, plugin deployment, StorageClass settings including device class, XFS or EXT4 filesystem, reclaim policy, ReadWriteOnce access, PVC reconstruction wait time, and project allocation.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/functions/using_volume_snapshot.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/functions/using_volume_snapshot.mdx): Explains how to create and consume VolumeSnapshots for PVC backup, restore, and rollback workflows. Covers prerequisites for Snapshot Controller and snapshot-capable dynamic CSI storage, a VolumeSnapshot CR example, console and CLI snapshot creation, creating new PVCs from snapshots, supported restore access modes for TopoLVM, CephRBD, and CephFS, and links back to PVC creation.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/how_to/configuring_persistent_localstorage.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/how_to/configuring_persistent_localstorage.mdx): Covers persistent storage using local volumes through the Alauda Build of LocalStorage operator. Explains installation from OperatorHub, static provisioning with `LocalVolume` resources that select nodes and device paths, StorageClass creation, filesystem or block mode, destructive wipe controls, verifying generated PVs, creating matching PVCs, and using `LocalVolumeDiscovery` and `LocalVolumeDiscoveryResult` to automate device discovery.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/how_to/configuring_persistent_storage.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/how_to/configuring_persistent_storage.mdx): Explains how to configure NFS-backed PersistentVolumes and PersistentVolumeClaims in ACP, including static PV manifests, binding PVCs by volume name, and verifying resources. Covers partitioned exports for quota enforcement, NFS security based on POSIX owner, UID, and supplemental groups, export and firewall settings for NFSv3 and NFSv4, root squash considerations, and why released NFS PVs should be replaced with new PV objects rather than reused.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/how_to/configuring_pvc_dr.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/how_to/configuring_pvc_dr.mdx): Provides a full disaster recovery workflow for application PVCs using Alauda Build of VolSync and CSI snapshots across primary and secondary clusters. Covers VolSync installation, rsync-tls Secrets, `ReplicationDestination` and `ReplicationSource` resources, scheduled and one-time synchronization, status checks, enabling DR for a stateful application, planned migration, failover with restored PVCs and local sync, and failback after the primary cluster recovers.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/how_to/generic_ephemeral_volumes.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/how_to/generic_ephemeral_volumes.mdx): Introduces Kubernetes generic ephemeral volumes as temporary per-Pod volumes dynamically provisioned from StorageClasses through CSI drivers. Includes a Pod example using `ephemeral.volumeClaimTemplate`, explains automatic PVC creation and deletion with Pod lifecycle, and compares the feature with `emptyDir` for use cases needing advanced CSI capabilities such as snapshots, resizing, encryption, or attachable non-node-local storage.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/how_to/third_mask.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/how_to/third_mask.mdx): Explains how to annotate third-party StorageClass capabilities for ACP by creating a `StorageDescription` ConfigMap in `kube-public`. Covers required labels, provisioner-keyed YAML data fields for snapshot support, volume modes, filesystem access modes, block access modes, UI behavior for PVC creation and snapshot operations, sample ConfigMap content, update flow, legacy compatibility, and troubleshooting empty or stale access-mode lists.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/how_to/using_empty_dir.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/how_to/using_empty_dir.mdx): Explains Kubernetes `emptyDir` volumes for temporary Pod-local scratch storage. Provides a Pod example that mounts `/data`, describes default disk-backed storage versus `medium: Memory` tmpfs, and summarizes characteristics such as starting empty, sharing across containers in the same Pod, deletion with Pod removal, node locality, and common use cases like caches, logs, working directories, and sidecar data sharing.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/intro.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/intro.mdx): Introduces Kubernetes storage in ACP as an abstraction layer for persistent and temporary data in containerized workloads. Summarizes volumes, PersistentVolumes, PersistentVolumeClaims, dynamic provisioning, automatic mounting, access modes, lifecycle management, multiple backend types such as local disks, NFS, and cloud storage, and the role of storage in high-availability, persistent, multi-tenant enterprise applications.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/trouble_shooting/detach_csi_volumes_non_graceful_shutdown.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/trouble_shooting/detach_csi_volumes_non_graceful_shutdown.mdx): Explains how to recover CSI-backed persistent volumes after a node fails without a graceful shutdown and Kubernetes cannot detach volumes automatically. It covers when to use the procedure, applying the node.kubernetes.io/out-of-service NoExecute taint, verifying eviction and detachment, and removing the taint after the node is repaired or replaced.
- [docs/en/configure/storage/trouble_shooting/recovery_pvc_expansion.mdx](docs/en/configure/storage/trouble_shooting/recovery_pvc_expansion.mdx): Covers manual recovery from a failed Kubernetes PVC expansion by preserving the underlying PV and recreating a claim with a supported size. The procedure includes switching the PV reclaim policy to Retain, deleting the original PVC, clearing claimRef, binding a new PVC with volumeName, restoring the reclaim policy, and checking StorageClass allowVolumeExpansion.

## costmanagement

- [docs/en/costmanagement/cost_management.mdx](docs/en/costmanagement/cost_management.mdx): Introduces Alauda Cost Management as a cloud-native billing and cost-control product for tracking and optimizing enterprise resource spending. It positions the feature as a way to support detailed resource management and links out to the external cost management site.

## developer

- [docs/en/developer/building_application/application_observability/event.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/application_observability/event.mdx): Shows developers how to inspect Kubernetes events for application workloads from the ACP Deployment details page. It explains the Events tab, resource event cards, resource type abbreviations, event reason and severity fields, occurrence timing, and the limitation of Kubernetes event TTL for historical analysis.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/application_observability/log.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/application_observability/log.mdx): Explains how to view and query live container runtime logs for a Deployment in the ACP console. It covers Pod and container switching, previous logs, line buffer sizing, wrapping, search, raw view, export, full-screen truncated-line viewing, and cautions about truncation, copy reliability, and Kubernetes log retention.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/application_observability/monitor.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/application_observability/monitor.mdx): Describes ACP monitoring dashboards for namespaces and workloads, including application, workload, pod, CPU, memory, network, disk throughput, and disk IOPS views. It explains the monitoring plugin prerequisite, namespace dashboard labels for custom dashboards, default Deployment monitoring tabs, custom workload dashboards, PromQL visibility, and top-15 pod display behavior.
