- [docs/en/developer/building_application/application_workloads/container.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/application_workloads/container.mdx): Covers Kubernetes containers in ACP, with emphasis on debugging and interacting with running workloads. It explains ephemeral containers, kubectl debug, web-console debug initialization, kubectl exec, file upload and download with kubectl cp, and console-based EXEC and file transfer from Applications or Pods.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/application_workloads/cronjob_create.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/application_workloads/cronjob_create.mdx): Explains Kubernetes CronJobs for scheduled, repeatable tasks such as backups or cleanup jobs and shows both CLI and ACP console creation workflows. It includes a batch/v1 CronJob YAML example, image prerequisites, cron and job configuration fields, references to shared Pod and container configuration, ad-hoc execution, status monitoring, and deletion.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/application_workloads/deamonset_create.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/application_workloads/deamonset_create.mdx): Explains DaemonSets for running one Pod on each eligible node, including common system-agent use cases and scheduling constraints for unschedulable nodes and taints. It provides CLI and ACP console creation steps, DaemonSet YAML with hostPath and tolerations, rolling update settings such as maxUnavailable, and workflows for viewing, updating, and deleting DaemonSets.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/application_workloads/deployment_create.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/application_workloads/deployment_create.mdx): Covers Kubernetes Deployments as the standard controller for stateless applications managed through ReplicaSets and rolling updates. It includes YAML and console creation, image registry prerequisites, update strategy settings, Pod scheduling and networking options, container configuration, storage mounts, health checks, rollout commands, scaling, rollback, deletion, and CLI troubleshooting for Deployments, ReplicaSets, Pods, logs, health checks, and resource limits.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/application_workloads/job_create.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/application_workloads/job_create.mdx): Introduces Kubernetes Jobs for finite tasks that run Pods to completion, with retry behavior controlled by backoffLimit and success tracking through completions. It provides a batch/v1 Job YAML example and commands for creating the job, watching job status, inspecting details, and viewing logs from the Pods created by the Job.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/application_workloads/pod.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/application_workloads/pod.mdx): Explains Pods as Kubernetes application instances and provides direct Pod YAML and management workflows. It covers kubectl commands for listing, describing, logging, exec, port-forwarding, and deleting Pods, plus ACP console views for details, YAML, configuration, logs, events, monitoring, resource requests and limits, aggregated Pod resource calculation for init containers and regular containers, and safe Pod deletion use cases.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/application_workloads/statefulset_create.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/application_workloads/statefulset_create.mdx): Covers StatefulSets for stateful applications that need stable network identities, per-replica persistent storage, ordered scaling, and ordered rolling updates. It includes StatefulSet and headless Service YAML, console creation with replicas, partitioned update strategy, volumeClaimTemplates, shared Pod and container configuration references, health checks, CLI scaling and rollout commands, PVC retention notes, and console view, update, and delete actions.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/build_application_architecture.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/build_application_architecture.mdx): Introduces the ACP application-building architecture and the main platform components used to develop and run containerized applications across hybrid environments. It describes Archon for global control-plane APIs, Metis for business-cluster webhooks and status synchronization, Captain controller manager for Helm chart lifecycle management, and Icarus as the RBAC-aware web management interface.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/concepts/application_type.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/concepts/application_type.mdx): Summarizes the application types available under Container Platform Applications. It distinguishes Custom Applications created from images, YAML, or code; Helm Chart Applications for chart-packaged Kubernetes resources; and Operator Backed Applications that use Operators for automated lifecycle management.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/concepts/custom_application.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/concepts/custom_application.mdx): Explains ACP Custom Applications as declarative Kubernetes-resource aggregates built from Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, and other native objects. It details Application and ApplicationHistory CRDs, componentKinds, selectors, descriptors, info references, status tracking, versioned lifecycle history, change diffs, rollback traceability, observability aggregation, and compatibility with kubectl, Helm, and GitOps workflows.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/concepts/understanding_environment_variables.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/concepts/understanding_environment_variables.mdx): Explains Kubernetes environment variables for injecting runtime configuration, secrets, metadata, and resource values into containers without rebuilding images. It covers static env values, ConfigMap and Secret references, fieldRef and resourceFieldRef sources, precedence, database and environment-specific examples, kubectl creation commands, microservice and multi-container patterns, naming, validation, and security best practices.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/concepts/understanding_parameters.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/concepts/understanding_parameters.mdx): Explains container parameters as Kubernetes args that override Dockerfile CMD while preserving ENTRYPOINT behavior. It includes examples for application settings, environment-specific deployments, database connection arguments, kubectl run and create usage, complex web-server and multi-parameter Pods, security guidance against passing secrets as args, ConfigMap-backed configuration, troubleshooting parameter override issues, init-container patterns, and Helm templating.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/concepts/understanding_startup_commands.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/concepts/understanding_startup_commands.mdx): Explains Kubernetes startup commands as container command overrides corresponding to Dockerfile ENTRYPOINT and how they interact with args. It covers custom startup, debugging, initialization scripts, shell-form commands, kubectl usage, complex examples such as wait loops and multi-step bootstrapping, best practices for signal handling and security, troubleshooting failed commands, and advanced patterns for init containers and Helm values.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/concepts/unit.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/concepts/unit.mdx): Defines Kubernetes-style resource units used in ACP configuration, especially CPU cores and milliCPU values and memory units such as Ki, Mi, and Gi. It helps users interpret resource requests, limits, and quota values consistently across workload and namespace settings.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/concepts/workload_types.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/concepts/workload_types.mdx): Summarizes the main workload types available in ACP and their Kubernetes purposes. It distinguishes Deployments for stateless services, StatefulSets for stable identity and storage, DaemonSets for node-level agents, Jobs for one-time tasks, CronJobs for scheduled tasks, and Pods as the smallest runnable unit.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/configuration/add_bucket.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/configuration/add_bucket.mdx): Explains how to add object-storage bucket configuration for an application in ACP so workloads can consume bucket access information. It covers the console workflow for creating or selecting bucket-related configuration and associating it with application resources.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/configuration/add_configmap.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/configuration/add_configmap.mdx): Explains Kubernetes ConfigMaps in ACP for storing non-sensitive configuration separately from container images. It covers creating ConfigMaps from key-value pairs or files, using them as environment variables or mounted files, console and YAML workflows, update behavior, and practical considerations for application configuration management.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/configuration/add_secret.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/configuration/add_secret.mdx): Explains Kubernetes Secrets in ACP for managing sensitive application data such as passwords, tokens, certificates, and image registry credentials. It covers Opaque, TLS, and image pull secret use cases, creation through YAML or console forms, mounting or exposing Secret data to Pods, base64 encoding requirements, and references from workloads or ServiceAccounts.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/create_applications/chart_app.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/create_applications/chart_app.mdx): Explains how to create Helm Chart applications in ACP from packaged charts. It covers selecting charts and versions, configuring release names, namespaces, values, and parameters, deploying through the console, and managing chart-based application lifecycle operations such as upgrades, rollback, and deletion through Helm-oriented workflows.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/create_applications/cli.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/create_applications/cli.mdx): Describes command-line application creation and management in ACP using Kubernetes and platform CLIs. It outlines prerequisites for cluster access, authentication, namespace targeting, and resource creation through YAML or commands so users can automate application delivery outside the web console.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/create_applications/image_app.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/create_applications/image_app.mdx): Covers creating a custom ACP application from a container image, including workload, Pod, container, Service, and Ingress configuration. It explains choosing Deployment, DaemonSet, or StatefulSet models; configuring replicas, rolling update settings, scheduling, network and DNS options, environment variables, commands, probes, storage mounts, Services and Ingresses, and application update, force update, delete, health-check, and volume reference behavior.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/create_applications/oam_app.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/create_applications/oam_app.mdx): Explains creating OAM Applications in ACP using the Open Application Model and KubeVela integration. It covers prerequisites, console creation from an image with labels and annotations, adding components and operational traits, and YAML-based creation or migration using OAM application definitions while avoiding naming conflicts.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/create_applications/operator_app.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/create_applications/operator_app.mdx): Explains Operator Backed Applications in ACP as application instances managed by Kubernetes Operators and OLM. It covers Operator automation, declarative reconciliation, lifecycle management, dependency resolution, CatalogSource, ClusterServiceVersion, InstallPlan, OperatorGroup, Subscription CRDs, console creation from a catalog with Custom Resource parameters, and troubleshooting with events and api-resources.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/create_applications/s2i_app.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/create_applications/s2i_app.mdx): Explains creating applications from source code using Source-to-Image in ACP. It identifies the Builds operator prerequisite, describes the console workflow for selecting Create from Code, points to S2I application parameter management, and notes that the resulting Deployment appears on the application detail page.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/create_applications/yaml_app.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/create_applications/yaml_app.mdx): Explains creating ACP applications from raw Kubernetes YAML for users who prefer direct resource definitions over forms or templates. It covers image pull prerequisites, nodeSelector precautions for mixed Linux and Windows clusters, console creation, and a sample Deployment plus Service manifest with replicas, labels, ports, and CPU and memory requests and limits.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/how_to/cronjob_rules.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/how_to/cronjob_rules.mdx): Explains how to write scheduled task trigger rules using crontab expressions in ACP. It covers local-time-to-UTC conversion with Beijing time examples, the five cron fields and their valid ranges, supported special characters such as comma, dash, asterisk, and slash, and common schedule examples.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/how_to/sa_imagepullsecret.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/how_to/sa_imagepullsecret.mdx): Explains how to attach an imagePullSecret to a Kubernetes ServiceAccount so Pods can pull images from authenticated private registries. It shows the kubectl patch command for adding imagePullSecrets, verification with kubectl describe serviceaccount, default ServiceAccount behavior, and checking new Pods with jsonpath.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/how_to/service_certificate.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/how_to/service_certificate.mdx): Explains ACP service serving certificates for automatic TLS certificate generation and root CA synchronization across namespaces. It covers administrator setup with Kyverno RBAC, cert-manager Certificate resources, ClusterPolicy rules for cloning and rotating service-root-ca and generating Service certificates from annotations, plus developer usage for annotating Services, verifying generated Secrets, and mounting serving and CA certificates into Deployments.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/create_namespace.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/create_namespace.mdx): Explains namespace creation within ACP project management and how namespaces inherit project and cluster quota boundaries. It covers console creation with cluster and prefixed namespace names, optional ResourceQuota, LimitRange, Pod Security Admission, labels, annotations, GPU quota unit rules, ac CLI project namespace creation, and kubectl YAML examples for Namespace, ResourceQuota, and LimitRange.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/import_namespace.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/import_namespace.mdx): Explains importing an existing namespace into an ACP project so it can be managed under project governance. It describes when to import namespaces, prerequisites, and the Project Management console workflow through Namespaces, the Create Namespace dropdown, Import Namespace, parameter configuration, and final import action.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/limit_range.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/limit_range.mdx): Explains Kubernetes LimitRange usage in ACP to set default, minimum, and maximum CPU and memory constraints for containers in a namespace. It includes a v1 LimitRange YAML example with defaultRequest, default, min, and max values, plus kubectl apply and kubectl create limitrange command-line workflows.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/manage_namespace.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/manage_namespace.mdx): Covers updating existing ACP namespace governance settings. It explains console and CLI workflows for modifying ResourceQuota objects, editing LimitRange defaults and maximums, and updating Pod Security Admission labels such as enforce, audit, and warn through Project Management or kubectl.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/remove_namespace.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/remove_namespace.mdx): Explains the difference between deleting a namespace and removing it from ACP project management. It warns about the effect of namespace deletion on contained resources and describes removal scenarios where the namespace is detached from the project without necessarily deleting Kubernetes resources.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/resource_quota.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/resource_quota.mdx): Explains namespace ResourceQuota concepts for controlling aggregate resource consumption in ACP. It covers requests and limits for CPU and memory, Pod count quotas, YAML examples, kubectl creation and verification, storage quotas for all PVCs or specific StorageClasses, hardware accelerator quota sources, and custom other-quota rules that avoid duplicate core quota keys.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/security_policies.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/security_policies.mdx): Explains namespace-level Pod Security Admission and Kyverno policy options in ACP. It covers enforce, audit, and warn modes; privileged, baseline, and restricted standards; namespace labels and exemptions; Kyverno policy samples such as restricted, anyuid, hostaccess, hostnetwork, nonroot, node-exporter, and privileged; plugin and feature-gate prerequisites; and applying policies through console samples or kubectl.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/uid_gid_assign.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/uid_gid_assign.mdx): Explains automatic UID and GID range assignment for ACP namespaces to enforce non-root Pod security. It covers enabling the feature with Restricted Pod Security Policy and the security.cpaas.io/enabled label, locating the security.cpaas.io/uid-range annotation, validating default runAsUser and fsGroup injection, and rejection behavior when a Pod specifies IDs outside the assigned range.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/update_member.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/update_member.mdx): Explains namespace member management in ACP, including importing project members, adding OIDC users, assigning namespace roles, and removing members. It details constraints around project membership and system users, bulk import and selected-user workflows, OIDC account caveats before first login or synchronization, and removal of namespace permissions.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/update_overcommit.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/namespace/update_overcommit.mdx): Explains namespace CPU and memory overcommit ratios in ACP as the relationship between container limits and requests. It covers NamespaceResourceRatio CRD fields, cluster inheritance versus custom ratios, examples for calculating requests from limits, CLI creation with kubectl apply, console update steps, virtual-node precautions, and the rule that changes affect only newly created Pods.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/add_cronhpa.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/add_cronhpa.mdx): Explains CronHPA for scheduled, time-based scaling of Deployments, ReplicaSets, or StatefulSets with predictable usage patterns. It covers monitoring prerequisites, the tkestack.io/v1 CronHPA YAML schema with scaleTargetRef and crons, schedule and targetReplicas fields, kubectl creation, ACP console configuration under Deployment elastic scaling, rule priority when schedules collide, HPA conflict considerations, crontab format, and cluster timezone behavior.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/add_hpa.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/add_hpa.mdx): Covers configuring Horizontal Pod Autoscaler for ACP workloads using CPU, memory, network, storage, and custom metrics. It explains HPA behavior, readiness effects, CLI YAML examples, web console setup, autoscaling/v2beta custom metric formats, scale policies, calculation rules, and operational cautions for scale-down and quota limits.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/add_vpa.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/add_vpa.mdx): Explains VerticalPodAutoscaler for recommending and applying CPU and memory resource requests and limits to ACP workloads. It covers VPA modes such as Off, Auto, Recreate, and Initial, plugin prerequisites, CLI YAML creation, web console configuration, recommendation review, pod recreation risks, and follow-up resource updates from recommendations.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/application_delete.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/application_delete.mdx): Summarizes native application deletion behavior in ACP, including deletion of the application and its directly contained Kubernetes resources. It also notes that deletion removes associations with Kubernetes resources that were not directly part of the application definition.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/application_rollouts/bluegreen_deployment.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/application_rollouts/bluegreen_deployment.mdx): Walks through blue-green deployments on ACP using Argo Rollouts to switch traffic between current and new application versions with minimal downtime. It includes required ACP, Kubernetes, Argo Rollouts, and project prerequisites plus Deployment, Service, Rollout YAML, workloadRef migration, activeService configuration, manual promotion, and verification with kubectl argo rollouts.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/application_rollouts/canary_deployment.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/application_rollouts/canary_deployment.mdx): Guides progressive canary delivery on ACP with Argo Rollouts, Gateway API traffic routing, and ALB as the gateway provider. It covers stable and canary Services, Gateway and HTTPRoute setup, DNS validation, Rollout canary steps, traffic weights, pausing and promotion, image patching, verification commands, and aborting a failed rollout.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/application_rollouts/install_argo_rollouts.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/application_rollouts/install_argo_rollouts.mdx): Provides installation steps for the Alauda Container Platform Argo Rollouts cluster plugin. It lists package download, upload, and cluster plugin prerequisites, then directs administrators to Marketplace and Cluster Plugins to install the plugin for rollout features.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/application_upgrade.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/application_upgrade.mdx): Explains how to update native applications by importing or removing Kubernetes resources so workloads, networking, storage, and configuration can be managed together. It covers association rules, image and runtime configuration updates, supported resource types, version snapshot benefits, deletion behavior, and UI steps for managing application resources.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/chart_upgrade.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/chart_upgrade.mdx): Describes status analysis and lifecycle guidance for legacy Chart or template applications, noting that independent template application management is being discontinued in favor of native applications. It explains initialization, validation, and sync status meanings, including failure reasons such as ChartLoadFailed, PermissionCheckFailed, DependenciesCheckFailed, and ChartSyncFailed.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/export.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/export.mdx): Covers exporting ACP native applications for migration, reuse, and GitOps workflows as Helm Charts, simplified YAML, full YAML, Kustomize files, or repository commits. It explains repository permissions, Chart metadata fields, local and template repository exports, YAML preview options, code repository export settings, pull request behavior, and follow-up creation of native or GitOps applications.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/handle_resource_errors.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/handle_resource_errors.mdx): Explains how ACP and Kubernetes handle node memory and disk exhaustion through kubelet eviction policies, resource reclamation, scheduling behavior, QoS ranking, and the OOM killer. It includes hard and soft eviction threshold YAML, eviction signals for memory.available, nodefs, and imagefs, system-reserved allocatable configuration, pressure transition periods, daemon set guidance, and a worked memory reservation example.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/healthcheck.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/healthcheck.mdx): Explains Kubernetes health checks for ACP applications, including liveness, readiness, and startup probes using HTTP GET, exec, and TCP socket actions. It provides probe YAML examples, web console parameter meanings, best practices for delays and thresholds, and troubleshooting steps using pod events, logs, manual endpoint tests, configuration review, resource checks, and network checks.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/hotreload.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/hotreload.mdx): Describes manually reloading application configuration after a mounted ConfigMap changes when the application cannot automatically pick up updates. It lists prerequisites for volume-mounted ConfigMaps without SubPath, shows web console steps from ConfigMaps to Workloads, and gives reload command examples for Nginx and Logstash.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/start_stop.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/start_stop.mdx): Provides web console steps for starting and stopping ACP applications from the Application detail page. It includes the confirmation flow for stopping an application after reviewing the prompt.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/status.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/status.mdx): Defines native application and Deployment status meanings in ACP, including Running, Partially Running, Stopped, Processing, No Computing Components, and Failed. It explains that application status counts refer to computing components and Deployment status counts refer to container groups or pods.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/version.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/operation_maintaining/version.mdx): Covers native application version snapshots and rollback in ACP. It explains automatic version history for UI updates, manual snapshots for API-driven changes, the retention limit of the latest six snapshots, snapshot creation from the Version Snapshot tab, distributing snapshots as Charts, and rolling back from Historical Versions.
- [docs/en/developer/building_application/working_with_helm_charts.mdx](docs/en/developer/building_application/working_with_helm_charts.mdx): Explains Helm and ACP Catalog concepts, including charts, releases, HelmRequest, ChartRepo, Captain, Charon, and Application synchronization through annotations. It provides CLI and UI workflows for preparing and packaging charts, obtaining API tokens, creating and deleting chart repositories, uploading charts and images, deploying and patching applications through ACP APIs, uploading templates, and deleting template versions.
- [docs/en/developer/faq.mdx](docs/en/developer/faq.mdx): Answers namespace import questions about why multiple ResourceQuota or LimitRange objects should be avoided. It explains how ACP merges multiple ResourceQuotas into a single default quota using minimum values, why Kubernetes validates quotas and limit ranges independently, and why non-default objects should be removed after import to avoid unpredictable quota behavior.
- [docs/en/developer/images/how_to/creating_images.mdx](docs/en/developer/images/how_to/creating_images.mdx): Covers container image creation best practices for ACP, including base image reuse, tag compatibility, single-process containers, exec-based wrapper scripts, temporary file cleanup, Dockerfile layer ordering, exposed ports, environment variables, password handling, avoiding sshd, and persistent volumes. It also introduces image metadata with Dockerfile LABEL instructions for better platform consumption.
- [docs/en/developer/images/how_to/managing_images.mdx](docs/en/developer/images/how_to/managing_images.mdx): Explains image management for ACP workloads, focusing on imagePullPolicy behavior and image pull secrets for secured registries. It describes Always, IfNotPresent, and Never policies, default behavior for latest versus versioned tags, creating pull secrets from registry credentials, linking secrets to ServiceAccounts, and specifying imagePullSecrets directly in pod or ArgoCD workflow YAML.
- [docs/en/developer/images/overview.mdx](docs/en/developer/images/overview.mdx): Introduces container image concepts used by ACP, including OCI-formatted images, containers, registries, repositories, tags, and immutable image IDs. It explains how images package application dependencies, how ACP orchestrates containers across hosts, how tags and SHA identifiers reference image versions, and how ACP Registry or external registries store image repositories.
- [docs/en/developer/node_isolation_strategy/architecture.mdx](docs/en/developer/node_isolation_strategy/architecture.mdx): Describes the architecture of ACP Node Isolation Strategy, implemented through the Container Platform Cluster Core component. It explains that nodes are allocated per workload cluster and project so containers created in a project are forcibly scheduled onto nodes assigned to that project.
- [docs/en/developer/node_isolation_strategy/concepts/node_isolation.mdx](docs/en/developer/node_isolation_strategy/concepts/node_isolation.mdx): Defines node isolation as separating cluster nodes so containers from different projects do not share the same node. The concept focuses on preventing cross-project resource contention and performance degradation.
- [docs/en/developer/node_isolation_strategy/functions/create_isolation_policy.mdx](docs/en/developer/node_isolation_strategy/functions/create_isolation_policy.mdx): Provides web console steps to create and delete node isolation policies for project-exclusive node allocation in ACP. It explains Project Exclusivity behavior, project and node selection constraints, one-policy-per-project and one-policy-per-node rules, scheduling effects for rebuilt pods, manual eviction considerations, and the result of deleting a policy.
- [docs/en/developer/node_isolation_strategy/intro.mdx](docs/en/developer/node_isolation_strategy/intro.mdx): Introduces Node Isolation Strategy as a project-level mechanism for exclusive or shared use of cluster nodes. It highlights benefits for allocating nodes to projects and scenarios requiring stronger resource isolation or protection from other projects consuming node capacity.
- [docs/en/developer/node_isolation_strategy/permissions.mdx](docs/en/developer/node_isolation_strategy/permissions.mdx): Lists the permission table placeholder for the Node Isolation Strategy feature using the acp-nodegroups function resource. It is intended to show role-based access requirements for viewing and operating node isolation features.
- [docs/en/developer/oam_applications/architecture.mdx](docs/en/developer/oam_applications/architecture.mdx): Explains OAM application architecture in ACP as a modular model for reducing deployment and operations complexity. It describes components, operational characteristics, application resources, scenario phases from component selection to post-operation management, multi-environment support, and role separation among developers, platform operations, and application operations teams.
- [docs/en/developer/oam_applications/concepts/component.mdx](docs/en/developer/oam_applications/concepts/component.mdx): Defines OAM components as reusable runnable units such as stateless web services or stateful services that encapsulate compute, networking, storage, and configuration needs. It describes built-in Web Service and Stateful Service components and their parameters for image selection, network access, environment variables, storage volumes, startup commands, and resource limits.
- [docs/en/developer/oam_applications/concepts/cue.mdx](docs/en/developer/oam_applications/concepts/cue.mdx): Introduces CUE as the language ACP uses to define custom OAM components and operational characteristics. It explains CUE syntax elements such as parameter, output, outputs, optional fields, defaults, interpolation, context values, loops, and conditions, plus required labels, kube-public namespace use, schematic fields, custom component annotations, workload definitions, and TraitDefinition fields such as appliesToWorkloads, workloadRefPath, conflictsWith, and podDisruptive.
- [docs/en/developer/oam_applications/concepts/trait.mdx](docs/en/developer/oam_applications/concepts/trait.mdx): Explains OAM operation and maintenance features, or traits, that add runtime capabilities to components across environments. It covers trait categories and examples including annotations, environment variables, JSON patches, health checks, host aliases, HPA auto-scaling, ingress, labels, lifecycle hooks, custom monitoring, pure ingress, resource requests and limits, and manual scaling with example YAML for each.
- [docs/en/developer/oam_applications/functions/alarm_function.mdx](docs/en/developer/oam_applications/functions/alarm_function.mdx): Covers monitoring and alerting for OAM application components in ACP. It describes seven-day monitoring charts for CPU, memory, network traffic, packet count, disk throughput, disk IOPS, and GPU metrics, then explains alert policy setup, alert handling, and administrator-managed notification policies such as email or SMS.
- [docs/en/developer/oam_applications/functions/storage_function.mdx](docs/en/developer/oam_applications/functions/storage_function.mdx): Explains how to add storage and configuration resources to existing OAM applications in ACP. It covers Application Resources tab steps, storage class and capacity choices, persistent storage characteristics, configuration dictionary and secret types, sensitive data options, and mounting resources as storage volumes or environment variable references.
- [docs/en/developer/oam_applications/functions/version_function.mdx](docs/en/developer/oam_applications/functions/version_function.mdx): Describes OAM application version comparison and rollback in ACP. It explains that UI and non-UI changes generate versions, then shows how to open a component Change Log, compare a historical version with the current version, and roll back to a selected version.
- [docs/en/developer/oam_applications/how_to/component_style.mdx](docs/en/developer/oam_applications/how_to/component_style.mdx): Guides administrators in customizing the display style of platform-specific OAM components or operational features using labels and annotations. It covers name, tag, and description annotations, required provider labels, a ComponentDefinition YAML example with CUE schematic content, cautions about deleting used definitions, and how users view the customized component style when creating OAM applications.
- [docs/en/developer/oam_applications/install_oamplugin.mdx](docs/en/developer/oam_applications/install_oamplugin.mdx): A minimal installation page for the OAM plugin section. It currently contains only the Install heading and does not provide detailed prerequisites, procedures, or configuration steps.
- [docs/en/developer/oam_applications/intro.mdx](docs/en/developer/oam_applications/intro.mdx): Introduces ACP OAM applications as a higher-level abstraction for container and Kubernetes resources that lets users focus on application delivery rather than infrastructure details. It explains the Open Application Model, why OAM helps compose distributed cloud-native applications, CUE-based extensibility, and comparisons with native applications, GitOps applications, and Helm template applications.
- [docs/en/developer/oam_applications/permissions.mdx](docs/en/developer/oam_applications/permissions.mdx): Provides a template-style permissions reference for the OAM applications module. It shows role-based access columns for platform administrators, auditors, project administrators, namespace administrators, and developers, with placeholder feature and action rows for view, create, update, and delete permissions.
- [docs/en/developer/oam_applications/quick_start/quick_start_builtin.mdx](docs/en/developer/oam_applications/quick_start/quick_start_builtin.mdx): Quick-start guide for creating an OAM application with built-in components and operational features. It targets new OAM users and walks through basic application metadata, selecting the Network Service component, entering a HelloWorld image, configuring TCP port 80 with internal and external access, keeping default traits, and validating the external access address.
- [docs/en/developer/oam_applications/quick_start/quick_start_custom.mdx](docs/en/developer/oam_applications/quick_start/quick_start_custom.mdx): Quick-start guide for creating an OAM application with custom components and operational traits. It shows administrator creation of a ComponentDefinition that accepts raw Kubernetes objects, a TraitDefinition that generates labels, then user creation of an OAM application from the custom component with Deployment and ConfigMap YAML, manual scaling, label traits, and an added NodePort internal route for browser access.
- [docs/en/developer/oam_applications/quick_start/quick_start_yaml.mdx](docs/en/developer/oam_applications/quick_start/quick_start_yaml.mdx): Explains how proficient users can create OAM applications directly from YAML for quick migration or one-click configuration. It lists prerequisites, cautions about changing application and component names when reusing exported YAML, and the basic ACP console flow for selecting Create via YAML and submitting application YAML.
- [docs/en/developer/operator/architecture.mdx](docs/en/developer/operator/architecture.mdx): A template architecture document for an Operator module rather than completed product documentation. It instructs authors to add architecture diagrams covering core components, data flow, deployment architecture, concept links, and explanatory text, with references to product documentation examples.
- [docs/en/developer/operator/concepts/example_concept.mdx](docs/en/developer/operator/concepts/example_concept.mdx): A template core concepts page for an Operator module. It prompts authors to define feature concepts, purpose, use cases, parameters, examples, and potentially separate complex concepts into dedicated in-depth articles.
- [docs/en/developer/operator/concepts/example_insight.mdx](docs/en/developer/operator/concepts/example_insight.mdx): A template in-depth concept article for explaining a complex Operator-related concept. It outlines sections for terminology, motivation, advantages, applicable scenarios, constraints, principles, configuration examples, important parameter descriptions, and references.
- [docs/en/developer/operator/functions/example_function.mdx](docs/en/developer/operator/functions/example_function.mdx): A template function guide for an Operator feature. It explains the expected distinction between function guides and how-to documents, recommends concept-driven coverage with detailed CLI instructions, and provides placeholder sections for function introduction, main features, advantages, modules, steps, parameter tables, and examples.
- [docs/en/developer/operator/how_to/example_howto.mdx](docs/en/developer/operator/how_to/example_howto.mdx): A template how-to document for practical Operator tasks. It describes how-to characteristics such as scenario orientation, practical end-to-end steps, prerequisites, operation results, learn-more links, and follow-up actions, with placeholders for task introduction, use cases, parameter descriptions, and verification.
- [docs/en/developer/operator/installation.mdx](docs/en/developer/operator/installation.mdx): A template deployment document for separately installed Operator modules. It prompts authors to document installation prerequisites, web console and CLI installation procedures, uninstallation order including instances, Operator, and CRDs, and custom configuration options.
- [docs/en/developer/operator/intro.mdx](docs/en/developer/operator/intro.mdx): Template guidance for writing product or module introductions in ACP documentation, covering what an introduction should include, why it matters, and how to separate product-level positioning from module-level responsibilities. It provides placeholder sections for overview, advantages, application scenarios, and usage restrictions.
- [docs/en/developer/operator/permissions.mdx](docs/en/developer/operator/permissions.mdx): Template guidance for adding module permission documentation using the `K8sPermissionTable` MDX component. It explains that authors should replace placeholder function names with real `FunctionResource` values so permission points can be generated automatically.
- [docs/en/developer/operator/trouble_shooting/example_trouble_shooting.mdx](docs/en/developer/operator/trouble_shooting/example_trouble_shooting.mdx): Template guidance for writing troubleshooting articles with problem symptoms, root cause analysis, diagnosis steps, cause-specific solutions, preventive measures, and related content. It emphasizes confirmed causes, clear operational steps, and appropriate handling of logs, screenshots, prerequisites, and precautions.
- [docs/en/developer/operator/upgrade.mdx](docs/en/developer/operator/upgrade.mdx): Template guidance for module upgrade documentation when manual intervention is required. It identifies expected upgrade content such as process, configuration, rollback, and version-specific upgrade guides when procedures differ significantly between releases.
- [docs/en/developer/overview.mdx](docs/en/developer/overview.mdx): Overview of ACP developer capabilities for managing cloud-native applications through the web console and CLI across namespaces with RBAC. It links major workflows for namespace management, application creation from images, catalogs, YAML, or S2I code, application operations, observability, and Kubernetes workload management.
- [docs/en/developer/quick_start/quick_start_app.mdx](docs/en/developer/quick_start/quick_start_app.mdx): Hands-on quick start for creating and exposing a simple containerized application from an image in ACP. It walks through namespace creation, image repository configuration, Deployment creation, NodePort Service exposure, and endpoint validation with an Nginx example.
- [docs/en/developer/registry/how_to/common_cli_command.mdx](docs/en/developer/registry/how_to/common_cli_command.mdx): Common CLI operations for Alauda Container Platform Registry using the `kubectl acp` plugin and standard container tooling. It covers logging in, granting image pull or push permissions to users and service accounts through role bindings, and pulling or pushing images to namespace-scoped registry paths.
- [docs/en/developer/registry/how_to/registry_data_restore.mdx](docs/en/developer/registry/how_to/registry_data_restore.mdx): Backup and recovery procedure for Alauda Container Platform Registry data stored in S3-compatible object storage. It explains how to extract S3 settings from the `ModuleInfo` resource, back up bucket data with S3 tools, restore data to a target bucket, patch registry `ModuleInfo` configuration, and verify catalog, tag, pull, and push access.
- [docs/en/developer/registry/how_to/use_registry_within_cluster.mdx](docs/en/developer/registry/how_to/use_registry_within_cluster.mdx): Guidance for using Alauda Container Platform Registry from workloads inside Kubernetes clusters. It recommends the internal service address `image-registry.cpaas-system.svc`, shows a Deployment using namespace-based image paths, explains cross-namespace pull access with `system:image-puller` role bindings, and lists verification and troubleshooting checks.
- [docs/en/developer/registry/install/install_via_web_ui.mdx](docs/en/developer/registry/install/install_via_web_ui.mdx): Web console installation guide for the Alauda Container Platform Registry cluster plugin, aimed at guided or proof-of-concept deployments with simple storage and networking needs. It covers Marketplace installation, options such as external exposure, IPv6, NodePort, LocalVolume or StorageClass storage, replicas, resources, and post-install verification.
- [docs/en/developer/registry/install/install_via_yaml.mdx](docs/en/developer/registry/install/install_via_yaml.mdx): YAML-based installation guide for production or highly customized Alauda Container Platform Registry deployments. It provides a `ClusterPluginInstance` template with ingress, TLS, storage, S3, resource, and service configuration, explains required fields and S3 credential secrets, and includes update and uninstall commands through `ModuleInfo` resources.
- [docs/en/developer/registry/intro.mdx](docs/en/developer/registry/intro.mdx): Introduction to Alauda Container Platform Registry as a built-in, highly available container image repository integrated with ACP authentication, authorization, and namespace isolation. It explains internal and external access, namespace-scoped image organization, RBAC-based push and pull permissions, and use cases such as lightweight deployments, edge clusters, and S2I workflows.
- [docs/en/developer/registry/upgrade/registry_plugin_upgrade_guide.mdx](docs/en/developer/registry/upgrade/registry_plugin_upgrade_guide.mdx): Upgrade guide for migrating Alauda Container Platform Registry from the old `internal-docker-registry` plugin in ACP v4.1 and earlier to the new `image-registry` plugin in ACP v4.2 and later. It provides separate procedures for S3, NFS, and local storage, including backup, uninstall, PV retention or release, data copying, new plugin installation, and cleanup of old Marketplace resources.
- [docs/en/developer/s2i/functions/s2i_application_management.mdx](docs/en/developer/s2i/functions/s2i_application_management.mdx): Operational guide for creating and managing applications from source code through ACP Source to Image capabilities. It explains repository selection, branch, tag, or commit handling, builder images for Go, Java, Node.js, and Python, Build and BuildRun resource creation, output image repository configuration, application networking, and rebuild operations.
- [docs/en/developer/s2i/how_to/s2i_howto.mdx](docs/en/developer/s2i/how_to/s2i_howto.mdx): Short walkthrough for creating an application from Java source code using Alauda Container Platform Builds. It uses the `https://github.com/alauda/spring-boot-hello-world` repository, the Build method, an image repository supplied by the administrator, application settings, target port `8080`, and post-create status verification.
- [docs/en/developer/s2i/install_builds/install_builds_operator.mdx](docs/en/developer/s2i/install_builds/install_builds_operator.mdx): Installation guide for Alauda Container Platform Builds and its Shipyard instance. It covers downloading and uploading Builds and Alauda DevOps Pipelines packages with `violet`, installing the Builds Operator from OperatorHub with cluster-wide manual upgrade settings, creating a Shipyard instance, and verifying that the Create from Code entry appears in the application creation flow.
- [docs/en/developer/s2i/overview/architecture.mdx](docs/en/developer/s2i/overview/architecture.mdx): Architecture summary for Source to Image in ACP, showing that Alauda Container Platform Builds orchestrates automated image builds from Git repositories and pushes results to a registry. It identifies the Builds Operator as the lifecycle manager and Tekton `TaskRun` resources as the Kubernetes-native execution layer.
- [docs/en/developer/s2i/overview/intro.mdx](docs/en/developer/s2i/overview/intro.mdx): Introduction to Alauda Container Platform Builds as an S2I and automated pipeline capability for turning Java, Go, Python, and Node.js source code into runnable container images. It covers builder-image concepts, visual build management, lifecycle benefits, Helm and GitOps integration, CI/CD use cases, and the requirement for Alauda DevOps Pipelines in OperatorHub.
- [docs/en/developer/s2i/overview/lifecycle_policy.mdx](docs/en/developer/s2i/overview/lifecycle_policy.mdx): Lifecycle policy reference for released versions of the Alauda Container Platform Builds Operator. It records version `v1.1.0`, its release date of 2025-08-15, and end-of-life date of 2027-08-15.
- [docs/en/developer/s2i/overview/releasenote.mdx](docs/en/developer/s2i/overview/releasenote.mdx): Release notes for the Alauda Container Platform Builds Operator, including supported ACP and Alauda DevOps Pipelines compatibility. Version `v1.1.0` is listed for ACP v4.1 and DevOps Pipelines v4.1, with notes on security vulnerability remediation and independent release capability.
- [docs/en/developer/s2i/upgrade/upgrade_builds_operator.mdx](docs/en/developer/s2i/upgrade/upgrade_builds_operator.mdx): Upgrade guide for Alauda Container Platform Builds. It instructs administrators to download and upload the new Builds and DevOps Pipelines packages with `violet`, migrate Alauda DevOps Tekton v3 to Alauda DevOps Pipelines when upgrading from v4.0 or earlier, and confirm the OperatorHub upgrade manually.

## devops

- [docs/en/devops/devops.mdx](docs/en/devops/devops.mdx): Brief overview of Alauda DevOps as a Kubernetes-native, container-based cloud application platform with unified workload scheduling and fine-grained multi-tenant permissions. It highlights orchestration of DevOps toolchains and compatibility with existing or future tools through an external DevOps documentation site.

## extend

- [docs/en/extend/chart_repo.mdx](docs/en/extend/chart_repo.mdx): Entry point for Chart Repository information in ACP extensions, directing users to the Helm Charts workflow used by the Catalog. The linked workflow covers ChartRepo and HelmRequest CRDs, creating local chart repositories through ACP APIs, uploading packaged charts, deploying or updating chart-based Applications, and managing template versions in the UI.
- [docs/en/extend/cluster_plugin.mdx](docs/en/extend/cluster_plugin.mdx): Comprehensive guide to ACP cluster plugins, including the `ModulePlugin`, `ModuleConfig`, and `ModuleInfo` CRDs, dynamic form configuration, publishing with `violet`, web console installation, and YAML installation in the global cluster. It includes examples for non-config and config plugins, affinity rules, lifecycle labels (`Core`, `Aligned`, `Agnostic`), verification commands, and upgrade behavior.
- [docs/en/extend/download_package.mdx](docs/en/extend/download_package.mdx): Guide to using the `violet` command-line tool to download packages from the Alauda Customer Portal. It covers installing `violet` on Linux, macOS, and Windows, logging in with credentials or access tokens, listing scenarios and packages, downloading core or app packages, checking download status, and importing application YAML for validation and downloads.
- [docs/en/extend/operator.mdx](docs/en/extend/operator.mdx): OperatorHub and OLM guide for ACP administrators covering Operator lifecycle management, source types, installation modes, approval strategies, namespaces, web console installation, and YAML installation on target clusters. It explains OLM CRDs such as `CatalogSource`, `ClusterServiceVersion`, `Subscription`, and `InstallPlan`, with manual and automatic install examples and advanced scheduling through Subscription node selectors and tolerations.
- [docs/en/extend/overview.mdx](docs/en/extend/overview.mdx): Overview of the ACP extension system for adding capabilities to Kubernetes clusters. It distinguishes OLM-based Operators from ACP cluster plugins for Chart-based extensions and explains how both extension types expand platform functionality for operational requirements and use cases.
- [docs/en/extend/upload_package.mdx](docs/en/extend/upload_package.mdx): Guide to uploading Operator, Cluster Plugin, and Helm Chart packages to ACP with the `violet` tool. It covers package inspection, listing, signature verification, image pushing, Artifact and ArtifactVersion creation, platform and registry parameters, standby global cluster precautions, multi-cluster uploads, and bulk package upload workflows.

## gitops

- [docs/en/gitops/gitops.mdx](docs/en/gitops/gitops.mdx): Brief overview of Alauda Container Platform GitOps as an Argo CD based, Kubernetes-native continuous delivery solution. It explains Git-driven synchronization of application and infrastructure manifests to target clusters, improving deployment velocity, reliability, security, and multi-cluster distribution.

## hardware_accelerator

- [docs/en/hardware_accelerator/hami.mdx](docs/en/hardware_accelerator/hami.mdx): Short introduction to Alauda Build of HAMi, the heterogeneous AI computing virtualization middleware formerly known as k8s-vGPU-scheduler. It positions HAMi as an all-in-one chart for managing and sharing heterogeneous AI computing devices among Kubernetes workloads.
- [docs/en/hardware_accelerator/npu/npu_operator/installation.mdx](docs/en/hardware_accelerator/npu/npu_operator/installation.mdx): Installation guide for Alauda Build of NPU Operator on Ascend NPU nodes. It covers online and offline prerequisites, supported OS and hardware, required packages and node labels, plugin upload through `violet`, component selection for drivers, Ascend Device Plugin, runtime, exporter, MindIO, and related services, plus validation with `npu-smi`, `npuclusterpolicy`, allocatable resources, a sample NPU workload, monitoring setup, and driver cleanup after uninstall.
- [docs/en/hardware_accelerator/npu/npu_operator/intro.mdx](docs/en/hardware_accelerator/npu/npu_operator/intro.mdx): Introduction to the NPU Operator for automating software management on Kubernetes nodes with Ascend devices. It explains how the operator installs and manages drivers, firmware, runtimes, MindCluster device plugins, scheduling, monitoring, and fault recovery components so AI training and inference workloads can run as containers on NPUs.
- [docs/en/hardware_accelerator/pgpu.mdx](docs/en/hardware_accelerator/pgpu.mdx): Brief overview of the Alauda Build of NVIDIA GPU Device Plugin. It identifies the plugin as a Kubernetes DaemonSet that exposes GPU counts, monitors GPU health, and enables GPU-enabled containers to run in the cluster.

## hyperflux

- [docs/en/hyperflux/about.mdx](docs/en/hyperflux/about.mdx): Overview of Alauda Hyperflux as an AI assistant for Alauda Container Platform. It explains that users can ask the chatbot for platform operation steps, troubleshooting help, and generated examples to improve operational efficiency.

## install

- [docs/en/install/global_dr.mdx](docs/en/install/global_dr.mdx): Global cluster disaster recovery guide for ACP using primary and standby global clusters with real-time etcd synchronization. It covers supported failure scenarios, excluded namespaces and data limitations, DNS and VIP planning, primary and standby installation requirements, encryption key copying, etcd Synchronizer plugin configuration, sync verification, failover steps, service restarts, package upload considerations, and routine consistency checks.
- [docs/en/install/installing.mdx](docs/en/install/installing.mdx): Step-by-step guide for installing the ACP `global` cluster from the Core Package. It covers extracting the installer, starting `setup.sh` with optional IPv6 mode, configuring installation parameters in the web UI, verifying app releases and Pods with `kubectl`, installing the Product Docs plugin, detailed parameter guidance for Kubernetes version, endpoints, certificates, image registry, networking, nodes, and cleanup of the installer container.
- [docs/en/install/overview.mdx](docs/en/install/overview.mdx): Overview of ACP Core installation, defining installation as deployment of the `global` cluster before creating or importing workload clusters and installing extensions. It explains preparation, execution, and verification stages, and introduces the Alauda Customer Portal as the official source for downloads, documentation, support, marketplace packages, and license management.
- [docs/en/install/prepare/download.mdx](docs/en/install/prepare/download.mdx): Preparation guide for downloading the ACP Core Package from the Alauda Customer Portal. It explains architecture choices for x86, ARM, and hybrid packages, the requirement to install Core before extension packages in ACP v4.1 and later, and the process for migrating a single-architecture installation to hybrid by syncing images and removing the `cpaas.io/node-arch-constraint` label if present.
- [docs/en/install/prepare/node_preprocessing.mdx](docs/en/install/prepare/node_preprocessing.mdx): Node preprocessing checklist for ACP global cluster installation. It lists supported OS and kernel versions, CPU architecture notes, the `init.sh` quick configuration script, required checks for grub parameters, kernel modules, SELinux, swap, SSH, DNS, routes, ports, packages, temporary mounts, conflicting container or Kubernetes software, filesystem cleanup, cross-node connectivity, unique hostnames, and clock synchronization.
- [docs/en/install/prepare/prerequisites.mdx](docs/en/install/prepare/prerequisites.mdx): Prerequisites and resource planning guide for installing an ACP global cluster. It covers deployment architectures for multi-cluster, single-cluster, and single-node setups, minimum CPU and memory guidance including ARM sizing notes, required VIPs, external IPs, domain names, certificates, bandwidth, latency, network segments, and load balancer forwarding rules for ports such as 443, 6443, 11443, and optionally 2379.

## networking

- [docs/en/networking/ingress_loadbalancing/ingress_loadbalance_with_envoy_gateway.mdx](docs/en/networking/ingress_loadbalancing/ingress_loadbalance_with_envoy_gateway.mdx): Guide to ingress and load balancing with Envoy Gateway and the Kubernetes Gateway API. It explains traffic flow from DNS and external load balancers through GatewayClass, Gateway, HTTPRoute, Services, and Pods, and covers LoadBalancer, MetalLB, NodePort, weighted routing, session affinity with `BackendTrafficPolicy`, TLS termination or passthrough, mTLS concepts, and troubleshooting common Gateway issues.
- [docs/en/networking/observability/cli-usage.mdx](docs/en/networking/observability/cli-usage.mdx): Usage guide for the NetObserv CLI as a standalone tool or kubectl plugin for short-term packet and flow troubleshooting. It covers copying `kubectl-netobserv` and `yq` from the controller manager pod, help commands, packet captures with pcapng export and Wireshark metadata comments, Geneve or VxLAN encapsulation options, TUI and background modes, flow captures with JSON and SQLite exports, cleanup, and offline analysis.
- [docs/en/networking/observability/operator-deployment.mdx](docs/en/networking/observability/operator-deployment.mdx): Operator deployment guide for continuous Network Observability collection with NetObserv, FlowCollector, and ClickHouse. It explains how to use an existing ClickHouse server or create one with the ClickHouse Operator, install the NetObserv Operator, create a ClickHouse authentication secret, and configure a FlowCollector with eBPF features, sampling, ClickHouse addresses, database, retention, batching, and timeouts.
- [docs/en/networking/observability/overview.mdx](docs/en/networking/observability/overview.mdx): Overview of ACP Network Observability powered by NetObserv. It explains eBPF-based packet and flow capture, use cases for continuous cluster-wide monitoring and short-term troubleshooting, the operator architecture with eBPF agents, flowlogs pipeline, Kubernetes metadata enrichment, and ClickHouse storage, plus the CLI architecture for exporting captured packets or flows.
- [docs/en/networking/observability/prerequisites.mdx](docs/en/networking/observability/prerequisites.mdx): Prerequisites for deploying Network Observability in ACP. It lists administrator access, the NetObserv Operator package in the fixed `netobserv-operator` namespace, a reachable ClickHouse server and credentials, minimum kernel version 5.14, Ubuntu `kernel.perf_event_paranoid` tuning, and downloading and uploading the operator package with `violet`.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/alb_monitoring.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/alb_monitoring.mdx): Explains how administrators access the ALB Status monitoring dashboard from Operation Center and filter metrics by cluster, namespace, and ALB name. It defines the ALB traffic, resource, Ingress, HTTPRoute, and Rule metrics shown for the last five minutes, including active connections, requests per second, error rate, latency, CPU, memory, network, disk I/O, QPS, byte rates, and P50/P90/P99 upstream response times.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/alb_with_ingress_nginx_annotation_compatibility.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/alb_with_ingress_nginx_annotation_compatibility.mdx): Catalogs which ingress-nginx annotations are supported, unsupported, or partially achievable by the deprecated ALB controller. The compatibility matrix maps annotations for authentication, CORS, redirects, timeouts, URL rewrite, OpenTelemetry, ModSecurity, upstream host handling, access logs, and session behavior to ALB feature documents or notes where ALB differs from ingress-nginx.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/auth.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/auth.mdx): Covers ALB authentication at global, frontend, rule, and Ingress path levels, including Forward Auth to an external auth service and Basic Auth using htpasswd-style Kubernetes Secrets. It explains supported ingress-nginx auth annotations, cookie and redirect handling, CR configuration inheritance across ALB, Frontend, and Rule resources, the ALB-specific auth-enable annotation, Basic Auth secret formats, and troubleshooting with ALB pod logs and X-ALB-ERR-REASON headers.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/bind_nic_in_alb.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/bind_nic_in_alb.mdx): Shows how to bind ALB listeners to a specific network interface instead of the default IPv4 0.0.0.0 and IPv6 :: addresses. It provides annotation and patch commands for embedded cluster ALBs and user-defined ALB2 resources, including how to include Alive VIP management in the NIC list and how to disable IPv6 when the selected NIC lacks an IPv6 address.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/cors.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/cors.mdx): Defines ALB support for Cross-Origin Resource Sharing at the Rule level. It lists the CRD fields for enabling CORS, allowed origins, and allowed headers, and clarifies that when CORS is disabled ALB forwards cross-domain handling to backend services.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/decision_making_for_performance_selection_of_alb.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/decision_making_for_performance_selection_of_alb.mdx): Provides sizing and deployment guidance for ALB in small, medium, large, testing, and core-application environments. It compares single versus multiple load balancers, high-availability replica counts, measured QPS baselines, memory considerations for large data transfers, and project isolation models using dedicated instances, shared instances, or port-based allocation.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/deploy_high_available_vip_for_alb.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/deploy_high_available_vip_for_alb.mdx): Explains two ways to provide a highly available VIP for ALB: a LoadBalancer Service created for container-network-mode ALBs, or an external load balancer device. It covers MetalLB annotations for fixed VIPs and address pools, as well as external load balancer planning items such as public or private IPs, DNS, health check ports, protocol, timeout, interval, and unhealthy threshold.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/header_modification.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/header_modification.mdx): Describes request and response header rewriting in ALB before forwarding traffic to backends or returning responses to clients. It documents ALB Ingress and Rule annotations for rewrite-request and rewrite-response, the JSON structures for setting, adding, removing, and variable-derived headers, ALB-provided variables such as first_forward_or_remote_addr, and examples for propagating Authorization from cookies and setting HSTS.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/http_redirect.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/http_redirect.mdx): Documents ALB HTTP redirect behavior at Frontend and Rule levels, where matched requests return 30x responses with Location headers. It defines redirect CRD fields such as code, host, port, scheme, URL, prefix_match, and replace_prefix, and maps ingress-nginx annotations for permanent redirects, temporary redirects, ssl-redirect, and force-ssl-redirect to ALB redirect configuration.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/l4_l7_timeout.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/l4_l7_timeout.mdx): Explains ALB L4 and L7 proxy timeout configuration through Frontend or Rule CR fields without requiring an Nginx reload. It defines proxy_connect_timeout_ms, proxy_read_timeout_ms, and proxy_send_timeout_ms, explains how each timeout behaves between upstream operations, and maps the equivalent ingress-nginx proxy timeout annotations.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/load_balancing_session_affinity_policy_in_alb.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/load_balancing_session_affinity_policy_in_alb.mdx): Summarizes ALB load balancing and session affinity algorithms for distributing traffic across backend pods. It explains round robin, source IP hash, cookie affinity, header affinity, and EWMA latency-aware balancing, then shows how to configure policies and required attributes through ALB Ingress annotations or Frontend/Rule serviceGroup fields.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/modsecurity.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/modsecurity.mdx): Explains how to enable ModSecurity WAF protection for ALB traffic with ingress-nginx-compatible annotations, ALB-specific annotations, or ALB/FT/Rule CR fields. It covers OWASP Core Rule Set support, transaction IDs, custom snippets, recommended rules, ConfigMap references, configuration inheritance from ALB to FT to Rule, and a YAML example that blocks requests matching a custom SecRule.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/otel.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/otel.mdx): Details how ALB integrates with OpenTelemetry tracing by exporting request traces to an OTel collector such as Jaeger. It covers ALB-level and Ingress-level configuration, trust of incoming spans, per-Ingress collector overrides, inheritance from ALB to Frontend to Rule, sampling strategies such as always on, always off, traceid-ratio, and parent-base, reported resource and span attributes, and a full Jaeger plus HotROD example deployment.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/tcp_http_keepalive.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/tcp_http_keepalive.mdx): Defines ALB TCP and HTTP keepalive behavior at the Frontend port level, emphasizing that keepalive is between clients and ALB rather than ALB and backends. It contrasts TCP socket probes with HTTP persistent connections, notes that Nginx reloads automatically on changes, and lists CRD fields for HTTP header timeout, request count, timeout, and TCP count, idle, and interval options.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/understanding_alb.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/understanding_alb.mdx): Introduces deprecated ALB as an OpenResty-based Kubernetes Ingress controller managed by ALB Operator, with ALB instances combining an OpenResty data plane and Go control plane. It walks through deploying the operator and an ALB2 instance, running a demo Ingress application, and explains how ALB, ALB Instance, ALB Leader, Frontend, Rule, Ingress, IngressClass, and Project namespace selection cooperate to route requests.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/url_rewrite.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/alb_operator/url_rewrite.mdx): Shows how ALB rewrites request URLs before forwarding them to backend services. It provides an Ingress annotation example using nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target with regex capture groups and an equivalent Rule CR example using dslx, rewrite_base, and rewrite_target so a request such as /prefix-x/abc reaches the backend as /abc.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/envoy_gateway_operator.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/envoy_gateway_operator.mdx): Explains ACP's Envoy Gateway Operator, which packages the upstream Envoy Gateway Helm chart and manages envoy-gateway instances through EnvoyGatewayCtl resources. It describes the relationship among EnvoyGatewayCtl, GatewayClass, Gateway, EnvoyProxy, envoy-gateway control plane instances, envoy-proxy data plane instances, Gateway API Route resources, and policies, then provides console and YAML installation steps and an advanced configuration example for enabling backend routing.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/ingress_nginx_operator.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/ingress_nginx_operator.mdx): Describes the Ingress Nginx Operator for deploying and managing ingress-nginx instances through IngressNginx custom resources. It covers marketplace installation, console-created configuration fields, YAML examples for all-namespace and namespace-selected ingress sharding, LoadBalancer exposure with MetalLB annotations, deployment settings, SSL passthrough, dual-stack support, and ACP-specific default differences from the upstream Helm chart.
- [docs/en/networking/operators/metallb_operator.mdx](docs/en/networking/operators/metallb_operator.mdx): Introduces MetalLB Operator as ACP's Kubernetes-native LoadBalancer implementation for bare-metal or on-premises clusters using Layer 2 ARP/NDP or Layer 3 BGP advertisement. It explains VIP, ARP, GARP, controller, and speaker concepts, high-availability leader selection and failover, capacity planning for address pools and nodes, CRDs such as IPAddressPool, L2Advertisement, BGPPeer, BGPAdvertisement, and BFDProfile, plus installation, upgrade, and address-pool constraints.
- [docs/en/networking/overview.mdx](docs/en/networking/overview.mdx): Provides a broad ACP networking overview covering service discovery, Kubernetes Services, load balancing, ingress routing, service mesh traffic management, network policies, mTLS, network observability, and the core components used for east-west and north-south traffic. It explains Gateway API concepts and role-oriented resources, compares Service, Ingress, and Gateway API for L4 and L7 use cases, recommends LoadBalancer Services through MetalLB for L4 and Envoy Gateway for Gateway API L7, and traces a sample external request through DNS, external load balancer, gateway, Service, and Pod.
- [docs/en/networking/security/admin_network_policy.mdx](docs/en/networking/security/admin_network_policy.mdx): Documents cluster-scoped AdminNetworkPolicy and BaselineAdminNetworkPolicy support, including feature-gate requirements, Kube-OVN limitations, alpha maturity, and the single default baseline policy rule. It provides YAML examples for priority, subject, ingress and egress actions, selectors, ports, node selectors, and IP ranges, then explains console creation fields and CLI application for enforcing cluster-wide network policy guardrails.
- [docs/en/networking/security/network_policy.mdx](docs/en/networking/security/network_policy.mdx): Explains namespace-scoped Kubernetes NetworkPolicy in ACP for isolating pod ingress and egress traffic through the CNI plugin. It notes that pods allow all traffic by default until selected by a policy, warns that hostNetwork pods are unaffected, provides a YAML example with pod selectors, namespace selectors, IP blocks, ports, and policyTypes, and describes the web console workflow for creating ingress and egress block or allow rules.
- [docs/en/networking/security/understanding_network_policy.mdx](docs/en/networking/security/understanding_network_policy.mdx): Compares Kubernetes NetworkPolicy with AdminNetworkPolicy and BaselineAdminNetworkPolicy in ACP's Kube-OVN policy evaluation model. It explains the priority order AdminNetworkPolicy, NetworkPolicy, then BaselineAdminNetworkPolicy, and contrasts namespace-scoped developer-owned workload segmentation with cluster-scoped administrator guardrails for cross-namespace and multi-tenant traffic enforcement.

## observability

- [docs/en/observability/event/event.mdx](docs/en/observability/event/event.mdx): Explains the Operations Center Events page for viewing significant Kubernetes resource events and detailed event records over a selected time range. It covers cluster switching, significant event summaries, resource event filters such as resource type, namespace, event reason, level, and resource name, and event-detail indicators that distinguish Normal from Warning events.
- [docs/en/observability/event/intro.mdx](docs/en/observability/event/intro.mdx): Introduces ACP event observability, which stores, queries, and visualizes Kubernetes event and operational state changes for resources such as clusters, nodes, and Pods. It explains how event analysis can identify abnormal resource causes and feed alert policies, and notes the dependency on ACP Log Essentials, ACP Log Collector, and ACP Log Storage plugins.
- [docs/en/observability/inspection/architecture.mdx](docs/en/observability/inspection/architecture.mdx): Describes the inspection and component health architecture implemented by Courier and monitoring components. It explains how inspection CRs are created in the global cluster, processed by Courier using metrics from each cluster, and written back for display, and how ModuleHealth and ModuleHealthRecord CRDs define component health checks backed by Kubernetes, Prometheus, or VictoriaMetrics data.
- [docs/en/observability/inspection/functions/health.mdx](docs/en/observability/inspection/functions/health.mdx): Explains the Platform Health Status page for viewing feature-level and component-level health across installed platform capabilities. It shows where users with management or audit permissions access the page, how feature cards indicate health or fault status, and how to expand details for clusters without a feature, clusters with installed features, and faulty components.
- [docs/en/observability/inspection/functions/inspection.mdx](docs/en/observability/inspection/functions/inspection.mdx): Covers ACP basic inspection operations, scheduled inspection configuration, downloadable PDF and Excel reports, and interpretation of the latest inspection results. It details risk and utilization views for clusters, nodes, pods, and certificates, including fault and warning criteria for cluster status, API server connectivity, resource usage, ETCD and control-plane pods, node readiness, inode and disk pressure, pod readiness, restarts, and certificate expiration.
- [docs/en/observability/inspection/intro.mdx](docs/en/observability/inspection/intro.mdx): Introduces the ACP Inspection module as part of observability for automated resource assessment, live inspection progress, risk visualization, and report generation. It explains how inspection helps reduce manual checks and identify anomalies across clusters, nodes, pods, certificates, and resource usage, and lists prerequisites for monitoring components and email notification server configuration.
- [docs/en/observability/log/intro.mdx](docs/en/observability/log/intro.mdx): Introduces the ACP Logging module for observability workflows that collect, store, and query application, container, and infrastructure logs. It highlights log collection, scalable persistence through Elasticsearch and ClickHouse, flexible querying, and the role of Filebeat and related components in troubleshooting, compliance, and operational insight.
- [docs/en/observability/monitor/architecture/architecture.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/architecture/architecture.mdx): Explains ACP monitoring architecture across metric collection, storage, querying, dashboards, alerting, notification, and real-time alert status. It traces how Prometheus or VictoriaMetrics operators, ServiceMonitors, Nevermore, Warlock, Courier, Erebus, ALB, Alertmanager, and ElasticSearch or ClickHouse cooperate to collect metrics, manage MonitorDashboard and PrometheusRule resources, evaluate alerts, dispatch notifications, and validate notification templates, groups, policies, and email server Secrets.
- [docs/en/observability/monitor/architecture/capacity_planning.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/architecture/capacity_planning.mdx): Provides capacity-planning baselines for ACP monitoring components at small, medium, and large cluster scales. It gives Prometheus and VictoriaMetrics sizing recommendations for nodes, double-container pod counts, ingestion rates, replicas, CPU limits, memory limits, disk capacity, and seven-day write volume for courier-api, Thanos Query, Prometheus, vmselect, vmstorage, and related components.
- [docs/en/observability/monitor/architecture/component_selection_suggestion.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/architecture/component_selection_suggestion.mdx): Compares Prometheus and VictoriaMetrics options for ACP cluster monitoring and helps select an installation architecture. It describes component roles, high availability, single-node and multi-cluster modes, long-term storage, resource efficiency, migration limitations, and recommends VictoriaMetrics for high-throughput, HA, long retention, and multi-cluster scenarios while positioning Prometheus for simpler single-cluster or existing ecosystem deployments.
- [docs/en/observability/monitor/concept.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/concept.mdx): Defines key ACP monitoring concepts including metrics, metric values, timestamps, labels, PromQL, built-in indicators, exporters, ServiceMonitor resources, alarm rules, alarm policies, notification policies, notification templates, dashboards, panels, data sources, and variables. It explains how these concepts fit into metric querying, alert configuration, notification formatting, and dashboard filtering.
