- [docs/en/observability/monitor/functions/manage_alert.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/functions/manage_alert.mdx): Describes alert management for platform administrators and business users, including built-in system policies, custom rules, templates, notifications, role-separated alert views, real-time alerts, history, and alert silencing. It covers UI and CLI creation for resource, event, log, and blackbox alerts using PrometheusRule annotations and labels, alert template workflows, notification repeat intervals, silence configuration annotations, and recommendations for writing focused, symptom-oriented alert rules.
- [docs/en/observability/monitor/functions/manage_blackbox.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/functions/manage_blackbox.mdx): Explains ACP blackbox probe management based on Blackbox Exporter for ICMP, TCP, and HTTP checks of external endpoints. It covers creating blackbox monitoring items, synchronization delay, alert policies for connectivity and HTTP status code metrics, custom Blackbox Exporter modules for POST, TLS, DNS, TCP, ICMP, and authentication scenarios, and CLI examples using Probe and PrometheusRule resources.
- [docs/en/observability/monitor/functions/manage_dashboard.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/functions/manage_dashboard.mdx): Documents ACP monitoring dashboard management as a Grafana-compatible, platform-integrated visualization system. It covers custom dashboards for platform and business views, Grafana JSON import, variables, groups, panel creation, built-in and native PromQL metrics, supported panel types, layout and dashboard operations, MonitorDashboard YAML structure, common query functions such as label_values and query_result, common variable definitions, and limitations for selected built-in workload metrics.
- [docs/en/observability/monitor/functions/manage_metrics.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/functions/manage_metrics.mdx): Explains how to inspect and manage metrics collected by Prometheus or VictoriaMetrics in ACP. It shows how to view component /metrics output, query stored metric names through the platform metrics API, list built-in indicators and their PromQL, alert, dashboard, and variable metadata, and integrate external application metrics using Service, Endpoint, ServiceMonitor, selectors, scrape intervals, authentication, and target verification.
- [docs/en/observability/monitor/functions/manage_notifcation.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/functions/manage_notifcation.mdx): Covers ACP notification management for alerting, inspection, and authentication workflows. It explains notification servers for WeChat Work, DingTalk, Feishu, email, and webhook or group bots, contact groups, custom webhook header Secrets, notification templates and variables, email formatting markup, notification rules, project-level notification isolation, and related Secret, NotificationGroup, NotificationTemplate, and rule configuration procedures.
- [docs/en/observability/monitor/how_to/infra_nodes.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/how_to/infra_nodes.mdx): Guides administrators in placing Prometheus or VictoriaMetrics monitoring workloads on dedicated infra nodes through plugin configuration rather than patching generated workloads. It covers console and YAML nodeSelector and toleration settings, storage and LocalVolume node-affinity considerations, troubleshooting workloads scheduled on general nodes or failing to schedule, and follow-up verification against capacity planning targets.
- [docs/en/observability/monitor/how_to/restore_prometheus.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/how_to/restore_prometheus.mdx): Explains Prometheus TSDB backup and restore for ACP Monitoring with Prometheus. It covers use cases, prerequisites, storage path defaults, kubectl cp backup from the prometheus container or backend storage, optional Admin API snapshot creation, restore by copying data back into /prometheus, LocalVolume shortcuts, TSDB directory layout, cache limitations, and recommendations such as Retain reclaim policy for PV storage.
- [docs/en/observability/monitor/how_to/restore_victoriametrics.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/how_to/restore_victoriametrics.mdx): Describes VictoriaMetrics monitoring data backup and recovery for ACP clusters. It identifies the vmstorage-cluster StatefulSet and default /vm-data storage path, states prerequisites and use cases such as disaster recovery and migration, and directs administrators to use vmbackup and vmrestore while verifying backup integrity, scheduling regular backups, testing recovery, and monitoring backup jobs.
- [docs/en/observability/monitor/how_to/special_network_card_name.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/how_to/special_network_card_name.mdx): Shows how to collect monitoring data from nodes with custom-named network interfaces that do not match the default eth, en, wl, or ww patterns. It instructs administrators to find the monitoring moduleinfo resource in the global cluster, add an ait/chart-cpaas-monitor valuesOverride for global.indicator.networkDevice with a custom regex, wait for propagation, and verify node network traffic, throughput, and packet charts.
- [docs/en/observability/monitor/install_monitor.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/install_monitor.mdx): Provides installation guidance for ACP Monitoring with Prometheus and ACP Monitoring with VictoriaMetrics through the console or ModuleInfo YAML. It covers prerequisites, plugin version discovery, storage types, scale configuration, replica behavior, agent-only VictoriaMetrics mode, cross-cluster VictoriaMetrics dependencies, node selectors and tolerations for infra placement, resource tuning fields, verification with moduleinfo labels, and access URLs for Thanos, Prometheus, Alertmanager, and VictoriaMetrics UI.
- [docs/en/observability/monitor/intro.mdx](docs/en/observability/monitor/intro.mdx): Introduces the ACP Monitoring module as an observability capability for metrics collection, dashboards, alerting, and notifications. It explains how integration with Prometheus and VictoriaMetrics helps administrators and operations teams monitor clusters, nodes, applications, and containers, detect issues proactively, deliver alerts, reduce downtime, and maintain infrastructure reliability.
- [docs/en/observability/overview.mdx](docs/en/observability/overview.mdx): ACP Observability is presented as an integrated platform capability for cloud-native visibility across synthetic monitoring, centralized logging, real-time metrics, alerting, and distributed tracing. It positions these four pillars as a unified way to diagnose performance issues, improve reliability, and understand user experience across applications.
- [docs/en/observability/tracing/architecture.mdx](docs/en/observability/tracing/architecture.mdx): ACP distributed tracing uses OpenTelemetry and Jaeger to collect, transmit, store, query, and visualize trace data. It explains the OpenTelemetry Operator, otel-collector, Jaeger Operator, jaeger-collector, jaeger-query, Elasticsearch storage, and the write/query flows from instrumented applications to Tracing UI.
- [docs/en/observability/tracing/concept.mdx](docs/en/observability/tracing/concept.mdx): Core tracing concepts are defined for ACP users, including telemetry, OpenTelemetry, spans, traces, instrumentation approaches, the OpenTelemetry Collector, and Jaeger. The content explains how code-based and zero-code instrumentation produce traces, metrics, and logs for backends such as Jaeger, Prometheus, and Elasticsearch-backed observability tools.
- [docs/en/observability/tracing/functions/query_trace_logs.mdx](docs/en/observability/tracing/functions/query_trace_logs.mdx): Trace log querying lets operators correlate business logs with distributed traces by using TraceID and Pod filters. It covers prerequisites for adding TraceID to application logs, default time-range behavior, filtering by pod, time, and query conditions, exporting logs as JSON or CSV, selecting display fields, and viewing surrounding log context.
- [docs/en/observability/tracing/functions/query_tracing.mdx](docs/en/observability/tracing/functions/query_tracing.mdx): The tracing query workflow helps users search microservice request paths by TraceID, service, labels, span duration, error spans, span type, and maximum result count. It explains how query results are analyzed with span lists, time-series waterfall charts, and span details such as service, duration, URL, tags, and raw JSON.
- [docs/en/observability/tracing/how_to/java_auto_tracing.mdx](docs/en/observability/tracing/how_to/java_auto_tracing.mdx): Java applications can be integrated with ACP tracing without source-code changes by using the OpenTelemetry Java Agent automatic injection mechanism for Java 8 or later. The guide shows Deployment annotations for `instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-java`, `SERVICE_NAME`, and `SERVICE_NAMESPACE`, then verifies injection through the init container and tracing waterfall data in Observability.
- [docs/en/observability/tracing/how_to/log_with_traceid.mdx](docs/en/observability/tracing/how_to/log_with_traceid.mdx): Backend developers can correlate application logs with distributed traces by extracting TraceID values from request headers and adding them to log output. The guide includes Spring Boot Log4j/Logback patterns using MDC `trace_id`, Python examples using B3 headers such as `x-b3-traceid`, and verification through the Tracing and Log Query pages.
- [docs/en/observability/tracing/installation.mdx](docs/en/observability/tracing/installation.mdx): Cluster administrators install ACP tracing by deploying the Alauda builds of Jaeger and OpenTelemetry Operators, creating a Jaeger instance backed by Elasticsearch, deploying an OpenTelemetryCollector, Instrumentation, ServiceMonitor resources, and enabling the `acp-tracing-ui` feature switch. The guide provides installation scripts, required Elasticsearch Basic Auth inputs, OAuth2 proxy and ingress setup, OpenTelemetry pipelines for traces and metrics, and uninstall commands for all tracing resources.
- [docs/en/observability/tracing/intro.mdx](docs/en/observability/tracing/intro.mdx): ACP Distributed Tracing provides end-to-end request tracking for microservice architectures through trace collection, Elasticsearch-backed storage, visualization, service dependency mapping, and querying by TraceID, service names, tags, and related conditions. It highlights OpenTelemetry standards, SDK or automatic injection integration, and the need to tune sampling rates to manage Elasticsearch performance and storage in high-load environments.
- [docs/en/observability/tracing/trouble_shooting/incomplete_trace.mdx](docs/en/observability/tracing/trouble_shooting/incomplete_trace.mdx): Incomplete trace results are attributed to Elasticsearch persistence delays for recent spans and Jaeger query time-range limits for traces longer than one hour. The troubleshooting guidance recommends refreshing after a delay or increasing `jaeger-query`'s `--es.asm.span-trace-query-time-adjustment-hours` parameter for long-running traces.
- [docs/en/observability/tracing/trouble_shooting/missing_trace.mdx](docs/en/observability/tracing/trouble_shooting/missing_trace.mdx): Missing traces in service mesh scenarios are linked to overly low sampling rates and Elasticsearch index refresh delays caused by the default `refresh_interval` of 10 seconds. The guidance recommends increasing sampling, considering tail sampling, or changing `jaeger-collector`'s `--es.asm.index-refresh-interval` parameter while noting Elasticsearch performance tradeoffs.

## overview

- [docs/en/overview/architecture.mdx](docs/en/overview/architecture.mdx): ACP is described as an enterprise Kubernetes platform with a hub-and-spoke architecture built around a `global` management cluster and multiple workload clusters. It covers global services such as gateway, authentication, web console, cluster management, OLM, registry, observability, and proxy; extension life cycles for Operator and Cluster Plugin stacks; and high availability mechanisms for etcd, monitoring, logging, CNI, ALB, VIP, Harbor, registry, and MinIO.
- [docs/en/overview/kubernetes-support-matrix.mdx](docs/en/overview/kubernetes-support-matrix.mdx): The Kubernetes support matrix maps ACP releases to Kubernetes versions supported for cluster creation and compatibility during global-cluster upgrades. It explains ACP 4.3 support for Kubernetes 1.34, third-party cluster management range `>=1.19.0 <1.35.0`, validated Extend baseline areas, and upgrade requirements that differ between ACP 4.3 and earlier releases.
- [docs/en/overview/release_notes.mdx](docs/en/overview/release_notes.mdx): ACP 4.3.0 release notes summarize new platform capabilities, including Kubernetes 1.34 support, CVO-based cluster upgrades, standalone Cluster Plugin upgrades, MicroOS-based global clusters on Huawei DCS, HCS and vSphere immutable-infrastructure work, next-generation console preview entry, containerd 2.0 baseline, and expanded third-party cluster management range. Fixed issues and known issues are placeholders pending later publication.

## security

- [docs/en/security/alauda_cluster_authentication.mdx](docs/en/security/alauda_cluster_authentication.mdx): The Alauda Cluster Authentication plugin provides independent authentication for workload clusters when the ACP `global` cluster is unavailable. It allows users to continue logging in and operating Kubernetes clusters with permissions consistent with the pre-failure global-cluster state.
- [docs/en/security/alauda_container_security.mdx](docs/en/security/alauda_container_security.mdx): Alauda Container Security is introduced as a Kubernetes and container security solution for centralized management, vulnerability scanning, policy enforcement, and compliance checks across multiple clusters. It uses Central Services for management, API, and UI plus Secured Cluster Services for monitoring, enforcement, and data collection, with integrations for CI/CD, SIEM, logging, and Scanner V4.
- [docs/en/security/audit/intro.mdx](docs/en/security/audit/intro.mdx): ACP auditing provides time-ordered user and system operation records for cluster and application troubleshooting, including what changed, who changed it, resource details, operation outcome, client IP, and timestamp. The guide explains prerequisites on logging plugins, filtering audit events by operator, action, cluster, resource type, and resource name, exporting CSV results, and viewing full audit JSON details.
- [docs/en/security/cert/cert-manager.mdx](docs/en/security/cert/cert-manager.mdx): cert-manager is explained as the Kubernetes-native certificate controller automatically deployed in ACP clusters to manage TLS certificate lifecycles through Certificate, Issuer or ClusterIssuer, and CertificateRequest resources. It describes key generation, CSR creation, certificate storage in TLS Secrets, automatic renewal, and labels or annotations that identify cert-manager-managed Secrets.
- [docs/en/security/cert/certificate-monitoring.mdx](docs/en/security/cert/certificate-monitoring.mdx): Cluster Enhancer monitors Kubernetes component certificates, TLS Secrets for in-cluster components, and certificates used by kube-apiserver endpoints. It exposes `certificate_expires_status` and `certificate_expires_time` metrics in cluster monitoring and provides built-in `cpaas-certificates-rule` alerts for Kubernetes and platform component certificates at 30-day, 10-day, and expired thresholds.
- [docs/en/security/cert/k8s-cert-rotator.mdx](docs/en/security/cert/k8s-cert-rotator.mdx): The Kubernetes Certificate Rotator cluster plugin automates rotation of Kubernetes certificates for supported On-Premises and DCS clusters. It lists covered control-plane and node certificates, explains metadata collection through temporary Pods, ConfigMap and Secret storage, rotation triggers based on remaining validity, queue-based scheduling, component restarts, rotation timelines, and manual renewal with `cert-renew` when kubelet is unhealthy.
- [docs/en/security/cert/olm.mdx](docs/en/security/cert/olm.mdx): OLM certificates for components such as `olm-operator`, `catalog-operator`, `packageserver`, and `marketplace-operator` are managed automatically by the system. When Operator CSVs define webhooks or API services, OLM generates and rotates the required certificates.
- [docs/en/security/cert/rotate-tls-certs-of-platform-access-addr.mdx](docs/en/security/cert/rotate-tls-certs-of-platform-access-addr.mdx): Platform access-address TLS certificates can be rotated by backing up the existing `dex-serving-cert` Certificate and `dex.tls` Secret in `cpaas-system`, deleting them, and creating a new TLS Secret from replacement certificate and key files. For ACP v4.0.x disaster recovery deployments, the procedure must be applied to both primary and standby global clusters.
- [docs/en/security/online_operations/install.mdx](docs/en/security/online_operations/install.mdx): ACP Telemetry supports online operations by collecting cluster telemetry data and uploading system metrics to Alauda Cloud for monitoring and analysis. The guide covers license and internet-access prerequisites, installation of the ACP Telemetry cluster plugin on the global cluster, enabling Online Operations in Platform Maintenance, and plugin uninstallation.
- [docs/en/security/platform_security_configurations/change_client_secret.mdx](docs/en/security/platform_security_configurations/change_client_secret.mdx): ACP platform OIDC client-secret rotation is performed by updating only the `client-secret` key in the `cpaas-system/cpaas-oidc-secret` Secret on the global cluster, after ACP 4.3 and compatible Agnostic plugins are in place. The procedure covers backup, SHA-256 verification without exposing plaintext, `kubectl patch` or edit methods, automatic synchronization to member clusters, component health checks for `frontend` and `apollo`, and rollback.
- [docs/en/security/platform_security_configurations/disable_pkce_plain_method.mdx](docs/en/security/platform_security_configurations/disable_pkce_plain_method.mdx): ACP v4.3 enables PKCE by default but may retain the insecure `plain` challenge method for backward compatibility until affected Agnostic plugins are upgraded. The hardening procedure locates the `OAuth2Client` for `alauda-auth`, removes `plain` from `codeChallengeMethods` so only `S256` remains, verifies plugin login flows, and provides a rollback patch if authentication fails.
- [docs/en/security/project/functions/create_project.mdx](docs/en/security/project/functions/create_project.mdx): Project creation allocates resources from one or more ACP clusters into an isolated tenant space for a team or business unit. The guide explains name restrictions and blacklist values, selecting healthy clusters, setting project quotas, and the warning that ACP automatically creates same-name namespaces in associated clusters that should not be modified manually.
- [docs/en/security/project/functions/manage_project.mdx](docs/en/security/project/functions/manage_project.mdx): Project administrators can update a project's display name and description or delete an unused project from the Project Management details page. Deleting a project releases its occupied cluster resources and requires entering the project name for confirmation.
- [docs/en/security/project/functions/project_cluster.mdx](docs/en/security/project/functions/project_cluster.mdx): Project cluster management lets administrators add clusters to a project with CPU, memory, storage, PVC, pod, GPU, and video-memory quotas, or remove clusters to reclaim resources. It describes scenarios for adding capacity or exclusivity, GPU quota unit rules for GPU-Manager, MPS, and pGPU resources, unlimited quota behavior, and cautions about removing abnormal clusters.
- [docs/en/security/project/functions/project_member.mdx](docs/en/security/project/functions/project_member.mdx): Project membership management covers importing users from existing platform member lists or adding OIDC usernames, then binding project, namespace, or developer roles. It explains constraints when OIDC IDPs are or are not configured, limitations for default administrators and current users, namespace-scoped role assignment, user source behavior after login or sync, and removing members to revoke permissions.
- [docs/en/security/project/functions/project_quota.mdx](docs/en/security/project/functions/project_quota.mdx): ProjectQuota extends Kubernetes ResourceQuota by capping the aggregate of namespace ResourceQuotas across all namespaces in a project. The guide explains admission-time enforcement, supported quota keys such as CPU, memory, pods, services, ConfigMaps, Secrets, and PVCs, planning strategies for shared project capacity, and troubleshooting quota-exceeded or scheduling failures.
- [docs/en/security/project/intro.mdx](docs/en/security/project/intro.mdx): ACP projects provide multi-tenant resource isolation across one or more clusters, while namespaces provide smaller isolated workspaces within a project for running workloads. The introduction explains project quotas, namespace quotas, the cluster-project-namespace hierarchy, and the rule that a namespace belongs to one project and draws quota from a single cluster.
- [docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/api_security/install.mdx](docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/api_security/install.mdx): ACP API Refiner installation can be performed from the Web Console or by creating a `ModuleInfo` for the `apirefiner` ModulePlugin on the `global` cluster. The guide explains checking ModulePlugin and ModuleConfig availability, required labels and version fields, uninstalling the plugin, default filtered resources, and desensitization of `metadata.annotations.cpaas.io/creator`.
- [docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/api_security/intro.mdx](docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/api_security/intro.mdx): ACP API Refiner enhances multi-tenant Kubernetes API security by filtering GET and LIST responses according to user permissions, projects, clusters, and namespaces, with support for field inclusion, exclusion, and desensitization. It documents required tenant labels such as `cpaas.io/project`, `cpaas.io/cluster`, and `cpaas.io/namespace`, plus limitations around OR label selectors, platform-level userbindings, and non-read operations.
- [docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/container_escape_prevention.md](docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/container_escape_prevention.md): Kyverno container escape prevention policies block privileged containers, host PID/network/IPC namespace sharing, hostPath mounts, host ports, risky Linux capabilities, and root execution. The guide includes enforce-mode ClusterPolicy examples, test Pods that should pass or fail, environment-specific strictness for production versus development, workload exceptions for system namespaces or monitoring workloads, and best practices such as starting in Audit mode.
- [docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/image_registry_validation.md](docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/image_registry_validation.md): Kyverno image registry validation controls which container registries may be used in Kubernetes workloads. It provides allow-list and deny-list ClusterPolicy examples for company registries, GCR, official Docker Hub images, Quay, environment-specific production and development rules, team-specific registry access, wildcard patterns, audit-first rollout, system namespace exclusions, and common image format pitfalls.
- [docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/image_signature_verification.md](docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/image_signature_verification.md): Kyverno image signature verification uses cosign keys, signatures, and `verifyImages` policies to block unsigned or improperly signed images. The guide covers key generation, signing images, public-key and certificate-based verification, digest mutation, multiple required attestors for critical images, environment-specific enforcement or audit behavior, and testing signed versus unsigned images.
- [docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/image_signature_with_secrets.md](docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/image_signature_with_secrets.md): Kyverno can reference Kubernetes Secrets that store cosign public keys instead of embedding keys directly in image verification policies. The guide covers creating Secrets from files, literals, or manifests; RBAC for Secret access; `verifyImages` policies using secret references; single-team, multi-team, multi-signature, and air-gapped offline verification patterns with Rekor and CT log options.
- [docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/network_security.md](docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/network_security.md): Kyverno network security policies restrict host network access, host ports, host port ranges, Service types, Ingress TLS and security annotations, custom DNS settings, and missing NetworkPolicies. It includes production and development rule variants, application-specific controls for database and web workloads, network tier labels for segmentation, and test manifests for failing and passing network configurations.
- [docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/private_registry_access.md](docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/private_registry_access.md): Private registry access configuration explains how Kyverno obtains credentials to inspect private images for signature verification, metadata checks, vulnerability scanning, and content validation. It shows creation of Docker registry Secrets in the `kyverno` namespace, adding them as `imagePullSecrets` to the Kyverno ServiceAccount, and optionally configuring the Kyverno Deployment with multiple image pull secrets and registry credential helpers such as `ecr-login`, `gcr`, and `acr-env`.
- [docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/security_context_enforcement.md](docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/security_context_enforcement.md): Kyverno security context enforcement policies require non-root users, block privilege escalation, constrain UID and group ranges, enforce seccomp and AppArmor profiles, require dropping Linux capabilities, and support read-only root filesystem patterns. The guide includes environment-specific, application-specific, graduated, and namespace-label-driven mutation examples that inject securityContext settings unless namespaces are labeled `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: privileged`.
- [docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/volume_security.md](docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/howto/volume_security.md): Kyverno volume security policies restrict unsafe volume types and mount behavior to prevent host filesystem access, privilege escalation, data exfiltration, and container escape. The guide covers safe volume allow-lists, hostPath blocking or read-only exceptions, privileged volume type denial, read-only root filesystems, sensitive mount-path restrictions, environment and application-specific volume rules, and EmptyDir size or memory-medium controls.
- [docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/install.mdx](docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/install.mdx): ACP Compliance with Kyverno can be installed from Marketplace or by creating a `ModuleInfo` for the `kyverno` ModulePlugin after confirming the available ModuleConfig version. The guide explains required cluster-plugin labels, module name, module type, version fields, and the console uninstallation workflow.
- [docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/intro.mdx](docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance/intro.mdx): ACP compliance functionality is based on Kyverno and lets organizations define YAML policies that validate Kubernetes resources against security, governance, and operational standards. It emphasizes custom policy enforcement, resource-level and policy-level violation views, violation monitoring, reporting, and remediation workflows for maintaining compliance posture.
- [docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance_service.mdx](docs/en/security/security_and_compliance/compliance_service.mdx): ACP Compliance Service is a platform module for STIG compliance scanning and MicroOS operating system scanning. It provides out-of-the-box compliance scans, scheduled scan execution, and reporting capabilities.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/group/functions/add_role.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/group/functions/add_role.mdx): Platform administrators can add roles to local or IDP-synchronized user groups from User Group Management and choose each role's cluster, project, or namespace permission scope. The guide notes that multiple roles may be assigned but each role can be added only once per group, and removing a role revokes all permissions granted to group members by that role.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/group/functions/create_group_howto.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/group/functions/create_group_howto.mdx): Explains how platform administrators create and manage local user groups for role-based access control. It covers the Users > User Group Management workflow, editable fields for local and IDP-synchronized groups, and deletion effects such as removing members and assigned roles.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/group/functions/manage_membership.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/group/functions/manage_membership.mdx): Covers importing and removing members in local user groups, including platform-management permission requirements and the 5000-member group limit. It explains that imported users inherit group roles and that removing a member revokes permissions granted through that group.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/group/intro.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/group/intro.mdx): Introduces ACP user groups as a way to grant or revoke platform, project, and namespace permissions for multiple users at once. It distinguishes local groups, which can be edited and have members managed, from LDAP or Azure AD IDP-synchronized groups, which can receive roles but cannot have their membership changed in ACP.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/idp/functions/ldap_manage.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/idp/functions/ldap_manage.mdx): Details LDAP identity provider integration for ACP, including OpenLDAP and Active Directory terminology, server connection parameters, user and group search settings, filters, and automatic synchronization policies. It also provides a Dex Connector YAML example, LDAP filter examples, manual user synchronization steps, and the effects of updating or deleting an LDAP connector.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/idp/functions/oidc_manage.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/idp/functions/oidc_manage.mdx): Explains how to add, validate, update, and delete OIDC identity providers for third-party login through Dex. It covers issuer URL, client ID and secret, redirect and logout URLs, email-based user identity, YAML-based Connector configuration, scopes, claim mappings, group synchronization, custom claim extras, and cleanup behavior for users and groups after IDP deletion.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/idp/intro.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/idp/intro.mdx): Introduces ACP identity provider integration through Dex connectors, with LDAP for enterprise directory accounts and OIDC for third-party authentication. It summarizes how LDAP and OIDC allow external users to authenticate and synchronize into the platform, and notes that other Dex connectors require technical support.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/idp/trouble_shooting/delete_user.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/idp/trouble_shooting/delete_user.mdx): Troubleshoots the case where creating or synchronizing a user fails because a previously deleted local or IDP user with the same name still exists in deleted state. It provides kubectl cleanup commands for deleted IDP users and local users, including removing local password resources in the cpaas-system namespace.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/role/functions/create_role_howto.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/role/functions/create_role_howto.mdx): Shows how to create native Kubernetes `Role` and `ClusterRole` objects from ACP's Kubernetes Roles page. It covers rule fields such as API groups, resources, resource names, and verbs, optional YAML editing, RoleBinding or ClusterRoleBinding creation for users, groups, or ServiceAccounts, and kubectl verification commands.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/role/functions/manage_role_howto.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/role/functions/manage_role_howto.mdx): Explains role administration after the RBAC refactor, separating read-only platform role templates from editable native Kubernetes roles. It covers viewing platform roles, granting platform roles through the console or `UserBinding` YAML, updating or deleting Kubernetes roles, managing RoleBindings from role, user, or group views, and operational best practices.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/role/functions/roletemplate_howto.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/role/functions/roletemplate_howto.mdx): Describes custom platform role creation with `RoleTemplate`, `FunctionResource`, `ClusterRole`, and `UserBinding` concepts. It compares FunctionResource-based rules, ClusterRole aggregation labels, and native `customRules`, includes YAML examples and field references, explains the ACP v4.3 migration toward Kubernetes ClusterRole aggregation, and gives copy-and-trim examples for read-only roles.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/role/intro.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/role/intro.mdx): Introduces ACP role-based access control, including platform, project, namespace, and cluster role scopes used to control user and group permissions. It frames platform roles as reusable permission sets that administrators bind to identities to authorize operations across ACP resources.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/user/functions/add_role.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/user/functions/add_role.mdx): Explains how platform administrators grant and revoke roles for users without modifying their own account. It separates Platform Roles, which bind predefined system templates once per scope, from Kubernetes Roles, which create native RoleBinding or ClusterRoleBinding objects against selected clusters, namespaces, roles, and subjects.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/user/functions/create_user.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/user/functions/create_user.mdx): Covers creating local ACP users from the console or by submitting an `auth.alauda.io/v1` `User` resource in the global cluster. It explains password generation and complexity requirements, unique mailbox or username behavior, validity periods, initial role assignment, post-creation navigation, and important User metadata labels and spec fields.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/user/functions/manage_user.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/user/functions/manage_user.mdx): Covers day-two user administration tasks, including resetting local passwords, changing account expiry, activating disabled or locked users, disabling users, adding local or LDAP users to local groups, viewing role tabs, deleting users, and batch operations. It also lists restrictions for system-generated accounts, the current user, and personal profile changes.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/user/intro.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/user/intro.mdx): Introduces ACP user authentication sources and lifecycle states. It describes local, LDAP, OIDC, and other Dex-authenticated users; explains username association rules across sources; distinguishes Platform Roles from Kubernetes Roles in user details; and defines Normal, Disabled, Locked, and Invalid account states.
- [docs/en/security/users_and_roles/user_policy/intro.mdx](docs/en/security/users_and_roles/user_policy/intro.mdx): Explains ACP user security policies used to harden login and account behavior. It covers enabling and disabling policy modules for dual authentication, password change enforcement, inactive-account disablement, failed-login locking, initial password notifications, session timeout, concurrent-login limits, browser tab behavior, and repeated-login prevention.

## service_mesh

- [docs/en/service_mesh/servicemesh-1.x.mdx](docs/en/service_mesh/servicemesh-1.x.mdx): Introduces Service Mesh 1.x as an Istio-based PaaS capability for microservice governance on ACP. It highlights one-click infrastructure deployment and upgrades, visualized service governance, application performance management, high-availability gateway management, and an external documentation entry point.
- [docs/en/service_mesh/servicemesh-2.x.mdx](docs/en/service_mesh/servicemesh-2.x.mdx): Introduces Alauda Service Mesh 2.x, built on Istio and installed through an Istio Operator derived from the Sail Operator. It explains the mesh as an infrastructure-level abstraction for existing applications, covering service discovery, load balancing, service-to-service authentication, failure recovery, metrics, monitoring, canary and A/B deployment, access control, and end-to-end authentication.

## storage

- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/architecture.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/architecture.mdx): Provides the architecture for Alauda Build of Rook-Ceph as ACP's Kubernetes-native storage layer for block, file, and object storage. It explains the roles of Ceph, Rook, Ceph CSI Operator, Ceph CSI, failure domains, OLM-installed `acp-storage-operator` and `rook-ceph-operator`, managed custom resources, daemon lifecycles, internal and external mode installation flows, and OLM upgrade behavior.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/concepts/concept.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/concepts/concept.mdx): Defines core Rook-Ceph concepts used by ACP distributed storage. It explains the Rook Operator, Ceph CSI, and major Ceph components including MON, MGR, OSD, MDS, RGW, RADOS, LIBRADOS, RBD, and CephFS, with each component's storage or operational role.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/functions/component_deploy.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/functions/component_deploy.mdx): Explains how to update node-specific deployment settings for Ceph components after distributed storage has been created. It covers fixed deployment for MON, MGR, MDS, and RGW on at least three selected nodes, limitations for Stretch clusters, service-impact precautions, and restarting components by deleting their pods or deployments so the system reschedules them.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/functions/device_class.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/functions/device_class.mdx): Covers adding custom device classes and devices to ACP distributed storage. It explains class naming restrictions, Standard versus Stretch cluster support, device selection through the Alauda Build of LocalStorage Operator, manual disk input, raw-disk recommendations, and storage disk status mappings such as IN+UP, IN+DOWN, OUT+UP, and OUT+DOWN.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/functions/monitor.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/functions/monitor.mdx): Describes ACP distributed storage monitoring and alerting capabilities for cluster health, capacity, performance, and component resource usage. It explains the Monitoring tab, metrics such as bandwidth, IOPS, latency, CPU, and memory, preset alert levels, notification strategy configuration, alert handling for Warning and Failure states, and fault review using alert history.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/functions/pool_management.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/functions/pool_management.mdx): Explains how to create, delete, and inspect Ceph storage pools from ACP. It covers file, block, and object pool types; replica count and device class selection; object storage settings such as region, S3 gateway, internal port, external NodePort access, and instance count; deletion prerequisites; and viewing object storage internal and external addresses.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/clean_ceph.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/clean_ceph.mdx): Provides a sequential cleanup procedure for deleting and redeploying a Rook-Ceph cluster. It covers removing VolumeSnapshotClasses, StorageClasses, storage pools, built-in pools, `CephCluster`, cleanup jobs, subscriptions, ConfigMaps, Secrets, and all namespace resources, then wiping host residuals with a `clean-rook.sh` script that removes `/var/lib/rook`, zaps disks, clears metadata, and verifies empty filesystem signatures.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/create_object_user.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/create_object_user.mdx): Shows how to create a `CephObjectStoreUser` CR for object storage access and customize display name, cluster namespace, quotas, and RGW capabilities. It also explains allowing users in namespaces outside the Rook cluster namespace and retrieving generated AccessKey and SecretKey values from the resulting Kubernetes Secret.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/dedicated_cluster.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/dedicated_cluster.mdx): Describes the recommended dedicated storage-cluster architecture for separating Ceph storage from business workloads. It covers platform and bare-metal requirements, MON/MGR/MDS/RGW/OSD CPU and memory sizing, disk count and capacity planning, 3-replica usable-capacity calculations, capacity alerts, scale-out recommendations, host-network public and cluster network isolation, 10GbE requirements, operator deployment, `CephCluster` YAML, and creating file, block, and object pools.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/disaster_recovery/dr_block.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/disaster_recovery/dr_block.mdx): Explains cross-cluster block storage disaster recovery with Ceph RBD Mirror and CSI-Addons VolumeReplication. It covers prerequisites, public-network requirements, enabling pool mirroring, exchanging bootstrap peer tokens, creating peer secrets, deploying `CephRBDMirror`, configuring csi-addons and `VolumeReplicationClass`, enabling mirroring per PVC, and planned migration, failover, and failback workflows using VolumeReplication states.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/disaster_recovery/dr_filesystem.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/disaster_recovery/dr_filesystem.mdx): Explains CephFS cross-cluster disaster recovery with asynchronous snapshot-based mirroring. It covers prerequisites for matching file pools, enabling mirroring on the secondary and primary clusters, obtaining a peer token, creating the primary-cluster peer Secret, configuring snapshot schedules and retention, deploying `CephFilesystemMirror`, and the requirement to restore Kubernetes PVC, PV, and workload resources before secondary-cluster failover.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/disaster_recovery/dr_object.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/disaster_recovery/dr_object.mdx): Explains Ceph RGW Multi-Site object storage disaster recovery using realms, zone groups, and zones. It covers creating primary and secondary object stores, configuring external NodePort access and custom endpoints, exchanging realm access and secret keys, pulling realm information to the secondary cluster, checking `radosgw-admin sync status`, and promoting zones for failover and failback with `radosgw-admin zone modify` and `period update`.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/in-transit-encryption.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/in-transit-encryption.mdx): Explains how to enable or disable Ceph `msgr2` in-transit encryption for ACP distributed storage through `CephCluster.spec.network.connections.encryption.enabled`. It lists ACP, kernel, and OS prerequisites, warns about CPU and latency overhead, gives new-cluster and existing-cluster patch examples, and provides verification, troubleshooting, and performance-benchmark guidance.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/persistent-volume-encryption.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/persistent-volume-encryption.mdx): Covers encryption at rest for RBD-backed persistent volumes using external KMS integration and encryption-enabled StorageClasses. It explains Vault token mode, alpha Vault tenant ServiceAccount mode, Thales CipherTrust Manager KMIP mode, KMS Secrets and `csi-kms-connection-details` ConfigMaps, encrypted StorageClass parameters, tenant Vault override ConfigMaps, and optional key rotation with csi-addons annotations and `EncryptionKeyRotationCronJob` control.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/pool_quota.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/pool_quota.mdx): Shows how to set logical Ceph storage pool quotas for file, block, and object pools. It provides kubectl JSON patch examples for `CephFilesystem.spec.dataPools[0].quotas.maxSize`, `CephBlockPool.spec.quotas.maxSize`, and `CephObjectStore.spec.dataPool.quotas.maxSize`, then validates applied limits with `ceph osd pool ls detail | grep max_bytes`.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/replacing-or-removing-devices.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/replacing-or-removing-devices.mdx): Provides a controlled procedure for replacing or removing a disk from a Rook-Ceph cluster. It covers prerequisites such as `useAllDevices: false`, capacity checks with `ceph osd df`, adding a replacement disk if needed, scaling down the Rook operator, marking the OSD out, waiting for `HEALTH_OK`, editing `CephCluster`, deleting and purging the OSD, wiping disk metadata, restoring the operator, and verifying health.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/replacing-or-removing-nodes.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/replacing-or-removing-nodes.mdx): Explains how to remove a storage node from an ACP-managed Rook-Ceph cluster while preserving data safety. It covers identifying OSDs on the node, checking remaining capacity, adding a replacement node when needed, excluding the old node from component scheduling, marking all node OSDs out, waiting for rebalancing, removing node storage entries and OSD deployments, purging OSDs, and verifying `HEALTH_OK`.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/rgw-d3n-cache.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/rgw-d3n-cache.mdx): Explains how to enable Ceph RGW D3N local cache for hot object reads using NVMe or SSD storage on RGW nodes. It covers preparing a persistent XFS host path, adding `additionalVolumeMounts` and `rgwConfig` fields to `CephObjectStore`, setting cache size and path, checking Ceph configuration and RGW logs, and validating cached object files on the host.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/set-ceph-osd-full-thresholds.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/set-ceph-osd-full-thresholds.mdx): Explains how to adjust Ceph OSD capacity thresholds for nearfull, backfillfull, and full states in ACP distributed storage. It covers using `rook-ceph-tools` to inspect health and runtime values, changing thresholds with Ceph CLI or declaratively through `CephCluster.spec.storage.nearFullRatio`, `backfillFullRatio`, and `fullRatio`, verifying the applied values, and restoring thresholds after capacity recovery.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/update_parameters.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/how_to/update_parameters.mdx): Shows how to manually update Ceph optimization parameters after cluster creation when the ACP UI no longer exposes them. It instructs administrators to update the `rook-config-override-user` ConfigMap `.data.config`, set `rook.cpaas.io/need-sync` to true, apply live settings with `ceph tell [mon|osd|mgr|mds|rgw].* config set`, and start the `rook-ceph-tools` deployment by editing the CSV if needed.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/installation/access_storage_service.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/installation/access_storage_service.mdx): Describes deploying ACP distributed storage in external mode to consume an existing Ceph service instead of creating a local Ceph cluster. It covers mutual exclusivity with internal mode, required ACP Storage Essentials and Rook-Ceph packages, Ceph version and pool prerequisites, required ports, FSID, MON, admin key, and pool information gathering, operator installation, external Ceph access configuration, network mode selection, StorageClass creation, and follow-up PVC usage.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/installation/create_service_extend.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/installation/create_service_extend.mdx): Explains creation of an alpha Stretch type Rook-Ceph cluster spanning two data availability zones and one quorum zone. It defines quorum and data zones, describes MON, OSD, MGR, MDS, and RGW placement, failure and network-partition behavior, limitations such as replica-only pools and all-flash OSDs, strict latency and topology requirements, node labeling with `topology.kubernetes.io/zone`, and stretch-specific cluster and storage pool parameters.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/installation/create_service_stand.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/installation/create_service_stand.mdx): Covers internal-mode, Standard cluster deployment for ACP distributed storage. It includes package preparation, minimum node and blank-disk requirements, Containerd `LimitNOFILE` guidance, ACP Storage Essentials and optional LocalStorage Operator installation, deploying the Rook operator, creating a Standard cluster with device classes, snapshots, monitoring, host or container networking, optimization parameters, fixed component placement, and creating file, block, or object pools.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/intro.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/intro.mdx): Introduces Alauda Build of Rook-Ceph as ACP's in-cluster hyper-converged distributed storage based on Rook and Ceph. It summarizes automatic management, scaling, and repair; block, file, and object storage support; graphical deployment, snapshots, monitoring, alerts, multi-replica reliability, online expansion, hybrid disk deployment, and links to deployment planning.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/planning.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_ceph/planning.mdx): Provides a comprehensive planning checklist for ACP Ceph distributed storage deployments. It compares internal co-resident, internal dedicated-node, and external models; discusses node roles, security and encryption, resource and capacity sizing, failure domains, storage device planning, network and IPv6 requirements, regional DR and stretch-cluster choices, performance considerations, and next-step links for the selected deployment model.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/architecture.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/architecture.mdx): Describes ACP Object Storage with MinIO as a distributed, Kubernetes-native object storage system using erasure coding, server pools, and high-availability mechanisms. It explains the MinIO Operator, MinIO Peer, and MinIO Pool components, StatefulSet-based tenant deployment, server and volume layout, erasure sets, multi-pool expansion, and the reliability tradeoffs of adding pools.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/concepts/concepts.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/concepts/concepts.mdx): Defines core MinIO storage concepts for ACP, including Reed-Solomon erasure coding, data and parity shards, server pools, erasure sets, data distribution, redundancy models, and capacity-based write placement. It explains how objects are split and distributed across drives so the system can tolerate drive failures according to the configured parity ratio.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/functions/add_pool.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/functions/add_pool.mdx): Explains how administrators add an additional storage pool to an ACP Object Storage with MinIO cluster from Storage Management. It covers selecting underlying storage, choosing 4-16 storage nodes, sizing each PVC-backed storage volume, and notes that adding a pool briefly interrupts MinIO before automatic recovery.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/functions/monitor.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/functions/monitor.mdx): Covers ACP Object Storage with MinIO monitoring and alerting for cluster health, service status, capacity, I/O performance, request counts, and failed object requests. It explains built-in alert policies, notification setup through Operations Center, severity levels, live alert handling, and using alert history for post-incident analysis.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/how_to/disaster_recovery.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/how_to/disaster_recovery.mdx): Describes MinIO disaster recovery using remote backup or active-active deployment between primary and target clusters. It defines hot backup and city-level active-active scenarios, lists external access, protocol, DNS, and certificate prerequisites, and shows how to add or remove a target cluster with access keys and synchronization status tracking.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/how_to/minio-to-ceph-rgw-with-volsync.md](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/how_to/minio-to-ceph-rgw-with-volsync.md): Provides a MinIO-to-Rook Ceph RGW object migration runbook using the Rclone binary embedded in the Alauda Build of VolSync Operator image. It details the full, incremental, and cutover sync strategy, required VolSync version matching, CephObjectStoreUser creation, Kubernetes Job/Secret configuration, validation, and troubleshooting for ImagePullBackOff, OOMKilled, 403, endpoint formatting, and RGW pressure.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/installation.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/installation.mdx): Walks administrators through installing ACP Object Storage with MinIO, including deploying Storage Essentials, deploying the MinIO Operator, creating a cluster, configuring credentials, resource scale, storage pools, external access, HTTP/HTTPS, NodePort or LoadBalancer, and creating buckets with the MinIO `mc` client. It also documents storage pool redundancy factor and usable-capacity formulas.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/intro.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_minio/intro.mdx): Introduces ACP Object Storage with MinIO as an S3-compatible object storage service for unstructured data such as images, videos, logs, backups, and VM or container images. It highlights MinIO simplicity, high performance, scalability across smaller clusters, cloud-native Kubernetes alignment, and support for objects from KB sizes up to 5 TB.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_topolvm/functions/device_management.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_topolvm/functions/device_management.mdx): Explains TopoLVM local storage device management for mapping bare disks on cluster nodes into device classes. It covers adding storage nodes and storage devices from Storage Management, naming device classes such as hdd or ssd, and warns that whole disks rather than partitions must be used.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_topolvm/functions/monitor.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_topolvm/functions/monitor.mdx): Covers ACP TopoLVM local storage monitoring and alerting for read/write bandwidth, IOPS, latency, node-local capacity, storage cluster health, service status, and device type status. It explains default alerts, notification policies, alert severities, common causes such as NotReady nodes or non-raw/missing disks, and alert-history review for operational tuning.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_topolvm/how_to/backup_and_restore.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_topolvm/how_to/backup_and_restore.mdx): Describes backing up and restoring TopoLVM filesystem PVCs with ACP Data Backup for Velero and S3-compatible object storage such as Ceph or MinIO. It covers backup repository setup, labeling PVCs and pods with `velero-backup=true`, creating a Velero Schedule with `defaultVolumesToFsBackup`, verifying object data in MinIO, and restoring namespaces and PVC data.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_topolvm/how_to/lvcreate_option.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_topolvm/how_to/lvcreate_option.mdx): Shows how to configure striped TopoLVM logical volumes by setting `topolvm.io/lvcreate-option-class` in a StorageClass. It explains the built-in `striped-default` class using `--stripes=2` and `--stripesize=64`, prerequisites for multiple devices in a device class, and how to create a custom `LVCreateOption` for alternate striping.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_topolvm/how_to/remove-disk.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_topolvm/how_to/remove-disk.mdx): Provides risk-aware procedures for removing failed disks, damaged device-class volume groups, or whole TopoLVM storage nodes from ACP local storage. It includes scenario selection, stopping `topolvm-operator`, finding affected LVM logical volumes, PVs, PVCs, and `logicalvolumes.topolvm.cybozu.com`, deleting affected storage resources, editing `TopolvmCluster` and `lvmdconfig`, restarting the operator, and verifying cleanup.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_topolvm/installation.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_topolvm/installation.mdx): Walks through installing ACP local storage based on TopoLVM, including lvm2 prerequisites, uploading Storage Essentials and Alauda Build of TopoLVM packages, deploying the operator, creating a local storage cluster, adding nodes and device classes, optionally enabling snapshots, and creating a StorageClass. It explains parameters for filesystems, reclaim policies, ReadWriteOnce access, device classes, and project allocation.
- [docs/en/storage/storagesystem_topolvm/intro.mdx](docs/en/storage/storagesystem_topolvm/intro.mdx): Introduces TopoLVM as a Kubernetes CSI plugin for managing high-performance local disks and SSDs through LVM. It explains topology-aware scheduling, dynamic volume creation, deletion and resizing, Kubernetes PVC integration, and why TopoLVM is useful for latency-sensitive workloads such as databases and caches.

## ui

- [docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/ac-and-kubectl-usage.md](docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/ac-and-kubectl-usage.md): Compares ACP CLI `ac` with standard `kubectl`, explaining that `ac` keeps kubectl-compatible behavior while adding ACP platform authentication, session management, multi-cluster configuration, and platform-aware commands. It highlights intelligent routing of global resources such as Users and Projects to the global cluster and lists additional commands such as `ac login`, `ac logout`, `ac config use-session`, `ac config use-cluster`, `ac namespace`, and `ac config sync`.
- [docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/administrator-command-reference.md](docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/administrator-command-reference.md): Reference for ACP CLI administrator commands under `ac adm`, requiring cluster-admin or equivalent permissions. It includes examples for node maintenance with drain, cordon, and uncordon, certificate CSR approve and deny, project and namespace creation, RBAC role assignment for projects, namespaces, clusters, and Kubernetes roles, release metadata workflows, and upgrade status or request commands.
- [docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/configuration.md](docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/configuration.md): Explains ACP CLI configuration tasks, including enabling Bash and Zsh shell completion and using `ac login` to authenticate to an ACP platform and generate a multi-cluster kubeconfig. It covers exported kubeconfig use with `KUBECONFIG`, ACP-specific session metadata, cluster naming conventions, and security precautions for token-containing kubeconfig files.
- [docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/developer-command-reference.md](docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/developer-command-reference.md): Comprehensive reference for ACP CLI developer commands that mirror common Kubernetes workflows. It includes examples for annotating resources, discovering API resources and versions, applying and creating manifests, managing kubeconfig contexts and ACP sessions, copying files to pods, creating workloads, services, RBAC objects, secrets, quotas, and deleting resources.
- [docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/extending-with-plugins.md](docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/extending-with-plugins.md): Covers extending ACP CLI with plugins so custom executables can add subcommands to the `ac` command experience. It explains plugin discovery conventions, naming, execution behavior, and how plugins can support site-specific workflows beyond the built-in ACP and Kubernetes command set.
- [docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/getting-started.md](docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/getting-started.md): Introduces ACP CLI for terminal-based management of ACP platforms and clusters, including downloading OS-specific binaries, installing on Linux, macOS, or Windows, and verifying with `ac version`. It walks through interactive and parameterized `ac login`, environment-variable login for automation, switching clusters and namespaces, running basic resource commands, managing multiple platform sessions, and creating a test nginx Pod.
- [docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/managing-cli-profiles.md](docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/managing-cli-profiles.md): Explains ACP CLI profile and context management for multi-platform, multi-cluster use through `ac login`, `ac logout`, `ac config get-sessions`, `ac config use-session`, `ac config use-cluster`, `ac namespace`, and `ac config sync`. It details the ACP-enhanced kubeconfig structure, extension metadata, `acp:<session>:<cluster>` naming, and when to use manual kubectl-compatible config commands for advanced scenarios.
- [docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/upgrading-clusters.md](docs/en/ui/cli_tools/ac/upgrading-clusters.md): Describes ACP CLI upgrade workflows for creating `ProductManifest` metadata, viewing current versions and `availableUpdates`, requesting latest or specific cluster upgrades, and inspecting preflight and stage progress with `ac adm upgrade` and `ac adm upgrade status`. It explains `--to-latest`, `--to`, `--allow-explicit-upgrade`, `ac adm release import-manifest --version`, wait behavior, troubleshooting missing updates, and interpreting Ready, Reconciling, and Degraded states.
- [docs/en/ui/cli_tools/violet.mdx](docs/en/ui/cli_tools/violet.mdx): Briefly introduces the `violet` command-line tool used to upload packages downloaded from the Customer Portal Marketplace. It points users to the package upload workflow for detailed usage.
- [docs/en/ui/web_console/access.mdx](docs/en/ui/web_console/access.mdx): Explains how to access the ACP web console through its route URL, authenticate with supported identity providers such as local auth, LDAP/Active Directory, OpenID Connect, or OAuth, and navigate after login. It covers prerequisites, the Portal page, view switching, and core UI areas such as the left navigation, user menu, and help menu.
- [docs/en/ui/web_console/customization.mdx](docs/en/ui/web_console/customization.mdx): Describes web console customization options for users and administrators, including theme and language preferences, login page logo and background, Portal page title and images, platform logo/name/favicon, and view names. It also explains integrating external product URLs into the Portal and view switcher and links to custom left navigation configuration.
- [docs/en/ui/web_console/customize-left-navigation.mdx](docs/en/ui/web_console/customize-left-navigation.mdx): Explains how administrators customize the Administrator and Projects view left navigation by editing `custom-navconfig-platform` and `custom-navconfig-project` ConfigMaps in the global cluster's `cpaas-system` namespace. It shows how to hide, rename, relabel, re-icon, and add navigation items using `buildIn` and `customs`, and provides default navigation structure references with names, hrefs, gates, children, and icons.
- [docs/en/ui/web_console/overview.mdx](docs/en/ui/web_console/overview.mdx): Introduces the ACP web console as a role-oriented graphical interface for developers, platform administrators, and project administrators. It documents tested browser versions, warns that IE 11 is unsupported, and explains the Portal page, view switcher, Container Platform, Platform Management, Project Management, and overview pages for health, resource, event, and alert summaries.

## upgrade

- [docs/en/upgrade/overview.mdx](docs/en/upgrade/overview.mdx): Summarizes the ACP 4.3 CVO-based upgrade model, including global-first sequencing, workload cluster upgrade gating by the global tier Distribution Version, and the web console two-step RPCH review flow. It defines key concepts such as ClusterVersionShadow, Distribution Version, Preflight, available upgrade targets, `upgrade.sh`, and global DR roles for primary and standby global clusters.
- [docs/en/upgrade/pre-upgrade.mdx](docs/en/upgrade/pre-upgrade.mdx): Lists pre-upgrade preparation for supported ACP 4.0, 4.1, and 4.2 to 4.3 paths. It calls out required `/cpaas/minio` free space, workload cluster compatible Kubernetes version checks, offline core package and extension package downloads, `ac-get-app.sh` use, `violet` upload requirements, and special package prerequisites for TopoLVM and Log Essentials.
- [docs/en/upgrade/upgrade_global_cluster.mdx](docs/en/upgrade/upgrade_global_cluster.mdx): Detailed procedure for upgrading the ACP global cluster to 4.3 using the CVO workflow. It covers running `upgrade.sh` to prepare product images, CVO images, and plugin artifacts, registry handling, preflight checks and block handling, requesting the upgrade through the web console, `ClusterVersionShadow`, or ACP CLI, observing `cvsh` status, and the additional standby-then-primary sequence for global DR environments including etcd Synchronizer handling.
- [docs/en/upgrade/upgrade_workload_cluster.mdx](docs/en/upgrade/upgrade_workload_cluster.mdx): Explains workload cluster upgrades after the global tier reaches the target ACP Distribution Version. It covers prerequisites, global DR sequencing, running `bash upgrade.sh --cluster=<cluster> --preflight`, requesting upgrades from the web console or ACP CLI with `ac adm upgrade --cluster`, tracking `ClusterVersionShadow` status, and troubleshooting conditions, preflight checks, stages, and history.

## virtualization

- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/backup_recovery/functions/snapshot.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/backup_recovery/functions/snapshot.mdx): Explains virtual machine snapshot use in ACP Virtualization with KubeVirt, including offline snapshot prerequisites, volume snapshot support, CephFS examples, and limitations when multiple storage types of the same kind exist. It covers creating snapshots with descriptions, checking Ready status, rolling back VM settings and snapshot-capable disks, Retain reclaim policy considerations for LVM-backed disks, rollback conflict handling, and deleting snapshots safely.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/backup_recovery/functions/velero.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/backup_recovery/functions/velero.mdx): Provides a Velero-based backup and restore workflow for KubeVirt virtual machines in ACP, using S3-compatible BackupStorageLocation, Ceph RBD block storage, CSI data movement, and the kubevirt-velero-plugin. It covers editing the Velero deployment and node-agent, creating Backup and Restore resources, verifying DataUpload/DataDownload completion, cross-cluster restore, namespace mapping, and storage class mapping through a Velero plugin ConfigMap.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/backup_recovery/intro.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/backup_recovery/intro.mdx): Introduces ACP Virtualization with KubeVirt snapshot-based backup and restore capabilities for virtual machines. It frames snapshots as a disaster recovery and failure rollback mechanism for data loss, human error, or ransomware, while noting that VMs must be stopped and VM disk PVCs must use multi-node shared access mode.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/functions/add_image.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/functions/add_image.mdx): Explains how administrators add public virtual machine images for X86_64 and ARM64 Alpha architectures in ACP Virtualization. It covers operating system choices, image sources from registries, HTTP file servers, or S3 object storage, supported qcow2/raw KVM image addresses, authentication with username/password credentials, and project assignment options.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/functions/update_delete_image.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/functions/update_delete_image.mdx): Brief UI procedure for updating or deleting existing virtual machine images from Administrator > Virtualization Management > Virtual Machine Images. It directs users to the image row action menu and confirmation flow.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/functions/update_delete_secert.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/functions/update_delete_secert.mdx): Brief UI procedure for updating or deleting virtual machine image credentials from the Image Credentials tab in Administrator > Virtualization Management > Virtual Machine Images. It covers selecting the row action menu and confirming the update or deletion.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/how_to/vm_image_export.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/how_to/vm_image_export.mdx): Runbook for exporting a stopped virtual machine's system disk image with KubeVirt `VirtualMachineExport`, downloading `disk.img.gz` using the export token and export Pod IP, uploading the image to S3-compatible object storage with `mc`, and then adding it back as an ACP virtual machine image source. It includes required namespace, VM name, TTL variables, kubectl commands, curl download, bucket creation, and image address format.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/how_to/vm_iso_linux.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/how_to/vm_iso_linux.mdx): Detailed workflow for creating a reusable Linux VM image from an ISO using KubeVirt and CDI DataVolume upload instead of container image builds. It covers uploading the ISO through `cdi-uploadproxy`, creating a VM with ISO and rootfs DataVolumes, installing Linux through VNC, removing the ISO disk from YAML, configuring cloud-init, SSH, qemu-guest-agent, root password behavior, partition expansion commands, and exporting the final custom image.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/how_to/vm_iso_windows.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/how_to/vm_iso_windows.mdx): Detailed workflow for building a Windows VM image from ISO with KubeVirt, including uploading Windows and virtio-win ISOs as CDI DataVolumes, creating a VM with SATA CD-ROM disks and USB tablet input for VNC mouse alignment, installing Windows, installing virtio-win drivers, exporting the image, optionally enabling UEFI, TPM, Hyper-V features for newer Windows versions, and exposing RDP through a NodePort internal route.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/intro.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/intro.mdx): Introduces ACP Virtualization with KubeVirt image management, where virtual machine images are modeled as Kubernetes CRDs and imported through the UI. It highlights support for mainstream Linux and Windows operating systems, X86_64 and ARM64 architectures, registry, HTTP, and S3 sources, and QCOW2 or RAW image formats.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/permissions.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/image/permissions.mdx): Defines the Kubernetes permission table reference for virtual machine image template functionality through `acp-virtualmachineimagetemplates`. It is intended to document RBAC permissions required for image template operations in ACP Virtualization.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/installation.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/installation.mdx): Explains how platform administrators prepare and install ACP Virtualization with KubeVirt, including package download and upload, planning network and storage, enabling node virtualization on physical nodes, installing the operator in the `kubevirt` namespace, creating the HyperConverged instance with `spec.storageImport.insecureRegistries`, and optionally configuring CPU overcommit. It compares TopoLVM and Ceph storage tradeoffs and explains VM memory quota reservation guidance.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/network/functions/vm_network.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/network/functions/vm_network.mdx): Short networking entry page that links VM network tasks to related virtualization and networking procedures. It points users to configuring VM IP addresses, connecting directly by IP through Kube-OVN Underlay physical networking, and adding services for virtual machines.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/network/how_to/managedtap.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/network/how_to/managedtap.mdx): Explains how to enable IPv6 support for virtual machines using KubeVirt network binding mode with the ManagedTap plugin and Kube-OVN Underlay networking. It covers ACP and CNI prerequisites, enabling DHCP, IPv6 RA, and underlay-to-overlay interconnection on the Kube-OVN Subnet, selecting Network Binding in the web console, configuring guest IPv6 settings, and setting an IPv6 default route when RA is unavailable.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/network/how_to/vm_multi_nic.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/network/how_to/vm_multi_nic.mdx): Shows how to configure multi-NIC virtual machines with Kube-OVN and Multus in ACP 4.1 or later. It covers creating a NetworkAttachmentDefinition and matching Kube-OVN Subnet provider, adding additional interfaces and Multus networks to VM YAML, configuring the new NIC inside the guest, and hotplugging or hot-unplugging bridge or SR-IOV interfaces on a running VM.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/network/how_to/vm_networkpolicy.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/network/how_to/vm_networkpolicy.mdx): Explains how ACP virtual machines, which run through KubeVirt Pods, can use Kubernetes Network Policies to control ingress and egress traffic. It covers policy association methods, protocols, ports, remote selectors, IP exclusions, DNS allowances on TCP/UDP ports 53 and 1053, and verifies blocking access to www.example.com by excluding its IP from an egress whitelist.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/network/how_to/vm_sriov.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/network/how_to/vm_sriov.mdx): Provides an end-to-end SR-IOV configuration workflow for ACP virtual machines, including BIOS virtualization, IOMMU, vfio-pci, VF creation, DPDK device binding, Multus CNI, sriov-network-operator deployment, node labels, NIC support ConfigMaps, and DHCP or manual IP setup. It notes OS constraints around glibc 2.34, required charts and images, vendor/device/VF IDs, and verifies success by creating a VM with an SR-IOV auxiliary NIC and checking eth1 in VNC.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/network/intro.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/network/intro.mdx): Introduces ACP Virtualization networking with KubeVirt and Kube-OVN, emphasizing support for traditional VM networking requirements. It highlights full IPv6 support, static IP retention across VM restarts, and multiple network modes such as container networks and SR-IOV.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/overview/intro.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/overview/intro.mdx): Presents ACP's KubeVirt-based container-orchestrated virtual machine solution for enterprises modernizing from traditional VM architecture to Kubernetes. It explains how existing qcow2 or raw VM images can run on the container platform while preserving VM management habits, and summarizes product areas such as virtual machines, disks, snapshots, images, and key pairs.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/storage/functions/virtual_disk.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/storage/functions/virtual_disk.mdx): Covers lifecycle management for ACP virtual disks used as persistent VM data disks, including creating, mounting, expanding, unmounting, and deleting disks from the Web UI. It details volume modes, storage classes backed by PVCs, delete-with-VM behavior, online mounting for running VMs, stopped-state requirements for unmounting, and restrictions such as not deleting system disks.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/storage/intro.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/storage/intro.mdx): Introduces ACP Virtualization storage with KubeVirt, PVC, CSI, and CDI as the foundation for persistent VM disk data and disk initialization. It emphasizes Web UI operations and lifecycle controls such as deciding whether VM disks are deleted with their associated virtual machines.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/virtual_machine/functions/batch_actions.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/virtual_machine/functions/batch_actions.mdx): Explains batch operations for ACP virtual machines, including start, stop, restart, and delete from the Virtual Machines list. It specifies which VM states allow each operation and warns that forced restart or stop behaves like cutting power and can lose data not written to disk.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/virtual_machine/functions/create_virtual_machine.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/virtual_machine/functions/create_virtual_machine.mdx): Details how to create a single KubeVirt VirtualMachineInstance or a VirtualMachinePool-based group in ACP, covering prerequisites, images, specifications, storage, networking, initialization, health checks, and node affinity. It describes root and data disks, PVC-backed storage classes, DHCP or static IPs, bridge and NAT modes, auxiliary NICs including SR-IOV, SSH keys or passwords, immediate startup, GPU passthrough prerequisites, and update limitations after creation.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/virtual_machine/functions/virtual_management.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/virtual_machine/functions/virtual_management.mdx): Describes common ACP VM management actions after creation, including resetting the root password, updating SSH keys, changing specifications, live migration, editing NAT open ports, updating tags and annotations, adding internal routes, reinstalling the operating system, and configuring IP assignment. It includes restart requirements for changes to take effect and warns that OS reinstallation clears the system disk and snapshots.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/virtual_machine/functions/virtual_secret.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/virtual_machine/functions/virtual_secret.mdx): Explains how to create, update, and delete SSH key pairs for ACP virtual machines. It notes that only SSH key pairs are supported, keys can be manually imported or generated by the platform, generated private keys can be downloaded locally, and ACP does not store the private key.
- [docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/virtual_machine/functions/vm_monitor.mdx](docs/en/virtualization/virtualization/virtual_machine/functions/vm_monitor.mdx): Covers monitoring and alerting for ACP virtual machines across CPU, memory, storage, and network metrics. It explains where to view real-time VM metrics, how to configure metric or event alert policies with trigger conditions and severity levels, how to handle alerts, and how notification policies can send alerts through channels such as email or SMS.
